cards.front: What is the strict definition of product-led growth?
cards.back: The product itself is the primary driver of acquisition, activation, retention, and expansion — people find it, try it, get value, and upgrade largely on their own, without a salesperson in the loop (OpenView, 2016).

cards.front: What sharp edge does Wes Bush put on the PLG definition?
cards.back: PLG is a go-to-market strategy — a choice about how you acquire and convert customers — not a product-quality aspiration. "We care about the product" is not PLG; "the product does the selling" is.

cards.front: When does adding sales to a product-led motion work?
cards.back: On top of a working self-serve engine, to expand accounts the product already won (Elena Verna's rule). Sales added to a product that can't activate users just industrialises the leak.

cards.front: How should you read OpenView's PLG Index (product-led companies at a premium)?
cards.back: Directional, not destiny. It's built from the survivors, so it supports "growth that compounds through the product gets priced higher" without proving the motion fits your price, buyer, and time-to-value.

cards.front: What does "product-led growth team" mean in this course?
cards.back: A way of operating, not a job title or SaaS category: the product and its usage data are the engine of growth, and growth is an engineering discipline — instrumented, measured, iterated.

cards.prompt: The growth-hypothesis format:
cards.cloze: If we {{make this specific change}}, {{this named metric}} will move {{in this direction}}, because {{a reason grounded in something we observed}}.

cards.front: Why name the metric before you look, and why the "because"?
cards.back: Naming the metric in advance stops you declaring victory on whatever happened to move; the "because" forces the idea to come from your own data (a replay, a leaky funnel step) instead of a tactic you saw on Twitter.

cards.front: Is a four-person team too small for this operating model?
cards.back: The opposite — the smaller the team, the more leverage, because a small company can't afford unmeasured bets. A founder who can read a retention curve and ship a journey is the whole growth team.

questions.q: What are the three layers of the machine this course builds?
options: Acquire, convert, retain
See it (PostHog) → act on it (Hogsend) → own it (identity + ads)
Frontend, backend, database
Awareness, consideration, decision
questions.explain: See it — PostHog (Ch 1–4). Act on it — Hogsend (Ch 5–6). Own it — identity + ads (Ch 7–8). Three layers, one loop, run on a cadence (Ch 9–10).

questions.q: What's the one-line division of labour that keeps the system legible?
options: PostHog detects, Hogsend acts, you decide
Marketing owns it, engineering supports it
The tool decides, the human executes
PostHog sends, Hogsend measures
questions.explain: PostHog detects (events, identity, analytics); Hogsend acts (the journeys that reach users); you decide what matters. Hold that split and the system stays simple no matter how much you add.

questions.q: Why do the journeys fire their results back as events?
options: To bill per send
So the loop stays measurable — the action you took becomes data PostHog can read
To satisfy a compliance requirement
Because Hogsend can't send without them
questions.explain: Hogsend acts on the events PostHog measures, and fires the outcome back as events — so the effect of every journey is itself measurable, closing the loop rather than disappearing into a black box.

questions.q: What do the last two chapters (9 and 10) add on top of the three layers?
options: Two more tools to install
The operation: the cadence it runs on, and the 30/60/90/180-day plan that sequences the build
A pricing model and a sales script
Nothing new — they're a recap
questions.explain: Chapter 9 is the rhythm (daily/weekly/monthly cadence + the experimentation program); Chapter 10 is the phased 30/60/90/180-day plan with checklists and exit criteria. They turn the machine into a habit, not a project.

flashcards:cards-plg-failures.title: Key ideas — how teams get it wrong

cards.front: The "dashboard graveyard" failure mode is what — and what's the fix?
cards.back: Analytics installed, nobody looks: the charts exist but no decision ever changed because of them. Measurement without a ritual or action is theatre. Fix: the daily cadence (Ch 4) plus the action layer (Ch 5–6).

cards.front: What's wrong with the "blast list" (a weekly newsletter to everyone)?
cards.back: Action without measurement — batch sends, no behavioural triggers, no holdouts. It feels like marketing and proves nothing. Fix: triggered journeys and incrementality testing (Ch 5).

cards.front: What's the "rented-audience trap"?
cards.back: All acquisition through paid ads, every reach re-bought, nothing captured into a channel you own. Stop paying and the pipeline goes dark. Fix: the owned-audience flywheel (Ch 8).

cards.front: Why is the "tactic collector" the most dangerous failure mode?
cards.back: It looks like doing everything right — constant shipping — but each tactic is copied, not derived from this product's data. The tell: nobody can say which leak the current project plugs. Tactics are the output of the loop, never the input.

cards.front: The through-line of all five failure modes?
cards.back: A growth team measures honestly, acts automatically, and owns its audience. Miss any one of the three and you've got a department, not a system.

cards.front: Why measure → keep → grow, in that order?
cards.back: Acquisition into a product that can't retain just buys churn. Retention is the highest-leverage, cheapest growth you have — plug the bucket before opening the tap. The order flexes with stage but never reverses.

cards.front: Why is acquisition the last lever, not the first?
cards.back: Pouring traffic into a product that can't keep users wastes the spend and hides the real problem. You open the tap only once the bucket holds water — otherwise every acquired user leaks straight back out.

cards.prompt: The three layers of the machine, and the division of labour:
cards.cloze: See it — {{PostHog}} (Ch 1–4). Act on it — {{Hogsend}} (Ch 5–6). Own it — {{identity + ads}} (Ch 7–8). The split that keeps it legible: PostHog {{detects}}, Hogsend {{acts}}, you decide.

questions.q: This course's definition of a 'product-led growth team' is:
options: A team at a self-serve SaaS company with a freemium tier
A team that operates on instrumented usage data — hypothesise, ship, measure, repeat — whatever its go-to-market
A dedicated growth department reporting to marketing
A team whose product has a viral loop built in
questions.explain: The strict definition (product as the primary acquisition/conversion driver) is a go-to-market choice that doesn't fit every business. The operating model — instrument, measure honestly, act, repeat — applies to almost any startup, and it's what the course installs.

questions.q: Your four-person startup sells a high-touch product to enterprises, so self-serve PLG isn't your motion. What does this chapter say applies to you anyway?
options: Nothing — this course is for self-serve products
Only the paid-ads material in Chapter 7
The operating model: instrument what users do, read it honestly, fix the real leak, measure whether the fix worked
You should switch to a freemium model first
questions.explain: PLG-as-go-to-market is a choice; product-led as an operating model is universal. Even a sales-assisted business has users doing measurable things, a funnel that leaks somewhere specific, and messaging that can be triggered by behaviour.

questions.q: Why does the course insist the messaging layer lives in code, in your repo?
options: Code-based emails render more reliably than drag-and-drop ones
It's cheaper than paying for a marketing SaaS
So the people who build the product own the layer that acts on its data — versioned, code-reviewed, and expressive enough for real logic
Because marketers shouldn't send emails
questions.explain: The operating model says growth work is engineering work. If the lifecycle layer is a config in a SaaS UI no engineer sees, the 'act on it' half of the loop is outside the team's discipline — unversioned, unreviewed, and capped at what the canvas exposes.

questions.q: The 'tactic collector' failure mode ships growth work constantly. What's actually wrong?
options: The tactics are copied from other companies instead of derived from this product's own data and constraint
The team ships too fast to measure anything
Referral programs and pricing experiments never work
The team lacks a dedicated growth hire to coordinate it
questions.explain: Tactics are the output of the loop (find the constraint → hypothesise → measure), never the input. A borrowed tactic can work — but only by luck, because it wasn't aimed at your leak. Elena Verna's 'tactics that never work' list is essentially a catalogue of this failure mode.

questions.q: Why is 'measure → keep → grow' in that order?
options: Measurement tools are the cheapest, so you buy them first
Acquisition into a product that can't retain just buys churn — retention multiplies every acquisition dollar, so plug the bucket before opening the tap
Ads platforms require analytics to be installed first
It's alphabetical in the original framework
questions.explain: The leaky-bucket argument (Chapter 2): pouring traffic into a product that loses users wastes the spend and hides the real problem. Retention work is the highest-leverage, cheapest growth available because those users are already yours.

questions.q: A founder tells you: 'We're four people — we'll adopt this operating model once we can afford a growth team.' What does the chapter say?
options: They're right — the rituals need a dedicated team to staff them
Partly right — install analytics now, but hold off on lifecycle messaging until the hire
They're wrong, but only if they're a self-serve SaaS
They're wrong — the smaller the team, the more leverage the model has, because a small company can't afford unmeasured bets
questions.explain: The evidence runs the other way: a four-person company can't afford a quarter spent building things nobody uses or ads that don't return, so the loop is cheapest exactly when you're small. Lovable ran this playbook to $200M ARR within a year with a team in the dozens.

questions.q: Your self-serve signups activate poorly, and someone proposes hiring salespeople to convert more of them. What's the chapter's warning?
options: Sales layered onto a product that can't activate users just industrialises the leak — fix activation first
Hiring sales means abandoning product-led growth entirely
Sales should never be added to a product-led company
Hire them, but keep them away from the self-serve accounts
questions.explain: Elena Verna's rule is that sales gets added on top of a working self-serve motion, to expand and enterprise-ify accounts the product already won. Reps pushing signups into a product that can't activate them scale the loss, not the revenue — the hybrid pattern works the same funnel, it doesn't patch it.

questions.q: A teammate adds 'try a referral program?' to next quarter's growth backlog. By the hypothesis format from this chapter, what's missing?
options: A budget estimate and a delivery timeline
Nothing — referral programs are a proven growth tactic
A named metric committed to in advance and a 'because' grounded in something observed in your own data
The A/B testing infrastructure to run it behind a flag
questions.explain: The format — if we [specific change], [specific metric] will move [direction], because [something observed] — makes each clause do work: naming the metric before you look stops you declaring victory on whatever moved, and the 'because' forces the idea to come from your data. A backlog of these is a growth program; 'try referral program?' is a wish list.

questions.q: Your team installed PostHog six months ago and the dashboards exist. How does the chapter say to test which rung of the maturity ladder you're really on?
options: Count how many events and dashboards you've configured
Ask whether any decision changed in the last month because of the data
Check that everyone on the team has a PostHog login
Compare your numbers against industry benchmarks
questions.explain: The most common self-deception is believing you're on rung three because a dashboard exists. If no decision changed because of it, rung two — analytics installed but no one looks — is closer to the truth, and that's the dashboard-graveyard failure mode.

questions.q: How does the chapter say to read OpenView's PLG Index, which shows product-led public companies trading at a premium?
options: As proof that any company adopting PLG will outperform the market
As irrelevant — public-market data says nothing about startups
As evidence that product-led companies are systematically overvalued
As directional, not destiny — an index of successful PLG companies can't count the ones the motion didn't suit
questions.explain: The Index is honest evidence with a selection-bias caveat: it's built from the survivors, so it supports the claim that growth compounding through the product gets priced at a premium without guaranteeing the motion fits your price, buyer, and time-to-value.

cards.front: What structural advantage does PostHog have over stitching point tools together?
cards.back: One event pipeline and one identity model under everything: the error links to the replay, the funnel, and the flag. Across four tools that join is a data-engineering project; in PostHog it's a click.

cards.front: The funnel tells you where you lose people — what tells you why?
cards.back: Session replay — real sessions behind a drop-off, with console logs and network requests captured. Five minutes of replay beats an hour of staring at a chart; the jump from a data point to its recordings is a click.

cards.front: Why can't GA4 run your activation funnel?
cards.back: GA4 answers aggregated, session-centric marketing questions (channels, campaigns). User-level funnels, N-day retention cohorts, and jump-to-replay need events keyed to durable persons — the product-analytics data model.

cards.front: How does this course split the work between PostHog and Hogsend?
cards.back: PostHog detects: event collection, identity, cohorts, the analytics you read. Hogsend acts: code-first TypeScript journeys in your repo — versioned, code-reviewed — triggered by the same events, results fired back as events.

cards.front: Which decision at PostHog signup is hard to undo?
cards.back: US vs EU cloud region. Data does not move between regions and you cannot merge across them — guessing wrong means a migration, not a setting. Pick where your users and compliance obligations are.

cards.front: Your phc_ project key is visible in the shipped JS bundle. Problem?
cards.back: No — by design it can write events but cannot read your data, like a mailbox slot. Reading requires a personal API key, which never ships to the browser.

cards.prompt: PostHog's pricing model in three facts:
cards.cloze: Usage-based per product, with monthly free tiers that {{reset each month}}; you're billed once {{at capture}} — storing history is {{free}}; roughly {{90% of companies}} use it for free. When cost arrives, the lever is event volume.

cards.front: Why PostHog Cloud for analytics but self-hosted Hogsend for messaging?
cards.back: The asymmetry is deliberate: analytics is read-heavy and fine to delegate; the messaging spine and the identity graph are assets you want on your own infrastructure.

questions.q: What's the single biggest structural advantage PostHog has over stitching Mixpanel + Hotjar + LaunchDarkly + Sentry together?
options: It's cheaper at every scale
Its individual products are each best-in-class
One event pipeline and one identity model under everything — the error, the replay, the funnel, and the flag are the same data about the same person
It's open source
questions.explain: Point tools can each be deeper in their niche. What they can't do is join: 'show me the replay behind this funnel drop-off' is a click in PostHog and a data-engineering project across four tools with four identity models.

questions.q: You already run GA4. Why can't it answer 'do users who create a project in week one retain better'?
options: GA4 has no event tracking at all
It's built for aggregated, session-centric marketing questions — not user-level funnels and retention cohorts keyed to durable persons
GA4 only works on mobile apps
It can, but only on the paid tier
questions.explain: GA4 technically has events, but the product answers channel/campaign questions over aggregated (and at scale, sampled) traffic. Retention-by-behaviour questions need every event tied to a durable person — the product-analytics data model, not the web-analytics one.

questions.q: Why does the course run analytics in PostHog Cloud but messaging in self-hosted Hogsend?
options: PostHog can't be self-hosted
Analytics is read-heavy and fine to delegate; the messaging spine and identity graph are assets you want on your own infrastructure
Hogsend doesn't offer a cloud version, so there's no choice
To avoid paying for two clouds
questions.explain: The asymmetry is deliberate. Losing an analytics vendor costs you charts; the identity graph and the ability to reach your users are the business. Own the assets, delegate the instruments.

questions.q: The most consequential decision in this chapter that you can't easily undo later is:
options: Which free-tier products to enable
US vs EU cloud region — data never moves or merges between them
Whether to install autocapture
Which dashboard template to start from
questions.explain: Nearly everything in PostHog is reversible — except the region. Getting it wrong means a migration, not a setting. Choose where your users (and your compliance obligations) are.

questions.q: Your `phc_` project API key ends up visible in your site's JavaScript. What's the risk?
options: An attacker can read your analytics data with it
Your account can be billed for someone else's events unless you rotate it weekly
Essentially none by design — it can write events but cannot read data; it's meant to be public
Session replays become publicly viewable
questions.explain: The project key is write-only by design, like a mailbox slot: anyone can post through it, nobody can read through it. Reading data requires a personal API key, which never ships to the browser (Chapter 3 returns to this split).

questions.q: A teammate proposes adding Segment in front of PostHog 'so we collect events properly'. Per this chapter, what is Segment actually for?
options: It's a pipe, not an analytics tool — it collects events once and fans them out to many destinations, and for a small team PostHog's own CDP plus direct capture usually cover it
It's a direct competitor to PostHog's product analytics
It's a required layer — PostHog can't ingest events without a CDP in front
It's a cheaper replacement for PostHog's session replay
questions.explain: Segment and RudderStack are complementary, not competitive — they can even feed PostHog. The chapter's rule: reach for a dedicated CDP when you have many destinations that need one canonical event schema; before that, it's overkill.

questions.q: Your funnel shows 41% of new users drop between signup and first project. What does the chapter suggest as the fastest way to find out why?
options: Ship an A/B test on the signup flow immediately
Add more events to the funnel until the drop explains itself
Export everything to a warehouse and model it in SQL
Watch session replays of the users who dropped — the qualitative layer under the quantitative funnel
questions.explain: The funnel tells you where; replay tells you why. Five minutes of watching real sessions behind a drop-off beats an hour of staring at a chart — and in PostHog the jump from data point to recordings is a click, not a project.

questions.q: What is the chapter's core argument for authoring lifecycle journeys in code rather than on a no-code canvas?
options: Code executes faster than canvas-based tools at send time
No-code tools can't trigger on PostHog events
Branching is ordinary TypeScript, a 48-hour wait is one line, and the whole journey lives in a pull request a colleague reviews — while canvas complexity compounds with every condition you add
Canvas tools are always more expensive per send
questions.explain: The example journey makes it concrete: an `if` and a `waitForEvent` replace a tangle of boxes, and the logic is versioned as a dependency you pin and code-review. Picture the canvas version with three more branches — that divergence is the whole argument.

questions.q: Which statement matches PostHog's pricing model as the chapter describes it?
options: You're billed once when an event is captured, storing historical events costs nothing, and each product's free tier resets every month
You pay a flat monthly seat price per team member
Historical events accrue storage charges the longer you keep them
The free tier is a one-time credit that never renews
questions.explain: The model is usage-based per product, and PostHog says roughly 90% of companies use it for free. When cost eventually arrives, the lever is event volume — which is why Chapter 3 tells you to be deliberate about what you track.

questions.q: You run a B2B product and want Group Analytics to roll events up to the company level. What does the chapter warn you to do first?
options: Nothing — Group Analytics is included in every product's free tier
Switch to the EU cloud, where Group Analytics is cheaper
Self-host, since Group Analytics isn't available on Cloud
Check the current pricing page — it's reportedly a paid add-on, and enabling it can cause all identified events in the project to bill, not just the grouped ones
questions.explain: This is one of the two things the pricing piece says to verify rather than take on faith, because it bites: the setting can change what your entire identified event volume costs, not merely add a line item for company rollups.

calc:ch2-retention-compounder.title: Feel the leak: retention vs. the pour

cards.prompt: The five AARRR stages, each as a question:
cards.cloze: {{Acquisition}}: how do people find you? {{Activation}}: do new users reach the value? {{Retention}}: do they come back? {{Referral}}: do they bring others? {{Revenue}}: do they pay, and how much?

cards.front: In the leaky-bucket model, what are the only two ways to raise the water level?
cards.back: The bucket is your business: water in = new customers, leaks = churn, level = active base. Pour faster (acquire more) or plug the holes (retain more) — and almost everyone reaches for the tap first.

cards.front: What do the mobile numbers say pouring into a leaky bucket costs?
cards.back: The average app loses ~77% of daily users within 3 days and ~90% within 30 (Andrew Chen's dataset; directional). Acquire 100 users at $5 with that leak and ~$385 is gone in three days — you bought churn.

cards.front: What is RARRA, and how does it differ from AARRR?
cards.back: Petit and Papp's 2017 reordering: Retention, Activation, Referral, Revenue, Acquisition. AARRR maps the user's journey; RARRA orders your priorities — "if you don't have retention, you have nothing."

cards.front: Why not copy Facebook's "7 friends in 10 days"?
cards.back: Magic numbers are population-level rally cries picked round on purpose — Facebook's could have been "10 friends in 12 days." Their real job is alignment; yours must come from your own data.

cards.prompt: The three-step method for finding your activation metric:
cards.cloze: {{Brainstorm candidate aha moments}}, {{run correlation analysis against retention}}, then {{run an experiment}} that pushes users to the candidate. Step 3 is the one teams skip — a good metric is causal, not just correlative.

cards.prompt: The four criteria of a good North Star Metric:
cards.cloze: It {{reflects customer value}}, {{expresses your strategy}}, is a {{leading indicator}}, and {{isn't directly movable}} — you work on 3–5 input metrics beneath it. Revenue fails: it's the lagging score, not the play.

cards.front: What is the one question to ask of a retention curve?
cards.back: Does it flatten above zero? A plateau means some cohort made the product a habit — that plateau is PMF expressed as data. Decay to zero means no amount of acquisition will save it.

calc:ch2-activation-value.title: What a point of activation is worth

questions.q: Your launch went well: signups tripled this month. Which AARRR question tells you whether that was worth anything?
options: Acquisition — which channel did they come from?
Activation and retention — did they reach the value, and are they still here?
Revenue — did MRR go up this month?
Referral — did they invite anyone yet?
questions.explain: Acquisition counts arrivals; the bucket sections are activation and retention. Tripled signups into a 90%-leak bucket is mostly money and attention set on fire — you can't judge the launch until you've watched the cohort activate and stick.

questions.q: Why does this course tell you NOT to adopt Facebook's '7 friends in 10 days' for your product?
options: It's outdated — the real number was recalculated later
Magic numbers are population-level rally cries, picked round on purpose; yours must be found from your own data and proven causal with an experiment
Social-network metrics never apply to other products
Seven is too ambitious for an early-stage product
questions.explain: Facebook's own people describe the number as a round pick from a range — its job was alignment, not precision. The method that matters is: candidates → correlation → experiment. Skipping the experiment risks optimising a number users hit on their way out the door.

questions.q: Users who watch a tutorial video retain at 71% vs 22% for those who don't. What have you learned?
options: The tutorial causes retention — push everyone to watch it
A correlation worth testing: engaged users might watch AND retain for the same underlying reason, so run an experiment that pushes more users to watch and see if retention actually moves
Nothing — retention splits like this are noise
The tutorial is your North Star Metric
questions.explain: This is exactly step 2 of the method — a strong correlation and nothing more. Only deliberately causing more of the behaviour (step 3) separates 'predictor of already-retained users' from 'lever that creates retained users'.

questions.q: Why is MRR a bad North Star Metric, by the framework's own criteria?
options: Revenue doesn't reflect customer value at all
It's too hard to measure accurately
It's a lagging output — by the time it moves, the game is played; the North Star should be the leading value-signal that causes revenue
Investors prefer ARR
questions.explain: A North Star must be a leading indicator you move through input metrics. Revenue is the scoreboard, not the play — PostHog picking 'Discoveries' (which predicted retention 2.3× better than alternatives) over any revenue count is the model to copy.

questions.q: Your retention curve falls for 30 days and then flattens at 12% forever. The strategic read is:
options: 12% is a failure — fix retention before anything else
A cohort has made the product a habit: evidence of PMF for that segment. Calibrate the plateau against category benchmarks, then decide if you're in 'fix the bucket' or 'open the tap' phase
Retention curves always flatten; it means nothing
Time to raise prices on the 12%
questions.explain: A plateau above zero — at any level — means some users stick indefinitely, which decay-to-zero curves never show. Whether 12% is good depends enormously on category (consumer vs B2B differ wildly), which is why you check operator benchmarks before reacting.

questions.q: You acquire 100 mobile app users at $5 each. Going by the dataset Andrew Chen popularised, what does the chapter say you should expect?
options: About 77 of them gone within three days — roughly $385 effectively set on fire before you've done anything wrong except scale a leaky bucket
About half churn in the first month, which is normal and safe to scale through
Nothing can be said until you have 90 days of retention data
Most will stick if the ads targeted the right audience
questions.explain: The chapter's math: the average app loses ~77% of daily users in three days and ~90% within 30. The figures are directional (one widely-cited dataset), but the lesson is structural — acquisition and retention multiply, so pouring into a leaky bucket is purchasing churn.

questions.q: A teammate reads about RARRA and declares AARRR obsolete. What's the chapter's actual position on the two?
options: AARRR is outdated; RARRA is the modern replacement and you should switch
RARRA is for mobile apps, AARRR is for SaaS
They contradict each other, so pick one and stay consistent
Same five letters, different jobs: AARRR maps the user's journey, RARRA orders your priorities — fix retention and activation first because that's where the leverage is
questions.explain: Petit and Papp reordered the acronym specifically to break the start-with-acquisition habit — 'if you don't have retention, you have nothing.' Nothing about the categories changed; what changed is what you fix first.

questions.q: Your 60 retained users sent 48 invites and 18 converted — a K-factor of 0.3. What does that number mean for your growth?
options: The product is viral: each user brings more users, so referral compounds on its own
The referral program failed and should be cut
Below 1, referral is a discount on CAC — real value, but each cycle shrinks; only above 1 does it compound into self-sustaining growth
K-factor only matters for consumer social apps
questions.explain: A K of 0.3 means each cohort brings in 30% of its own size — cheaper effective acquisition, which is genuinely worth having. But the loop decays every cycle, so it can't replace other acquisition until K crosses 1.

questions.q: You've used your funnel to find where users drop off. Now you want growth that sustains itself without constantly refilling the top. Which lens does the chapter hand you?
options: Stay with the funnel — loops are just funnels drawn in a circle
HEART, because it's the most granular framework
The PMF survey, to confirm fit before designing anything
Growth loops — a system where one cycle's output (a user creating shareable content) becomes the next cycle's input
questions.explain: Reforge's critique is that a funnel is linear — people fall out the bottom and you're always refilling the top. The funnel is the right lens for seeing leaks; the loop is the right lens for designing growth that feeds itself, like the owned-audience flywheel in Chapter 8.

questions.q: Before sinking months into activation optimisation, the chapter offers a cheap gut-check on whether you have product-market fit at all. What is it?
options: Ask users how they'd feel if they could no longer use the product — 40%+ saying 'very disappointed' suggests PMF
Check whether MRR grew last quarter
Ask users whether they'd recommend you (NPS above 50)
Compare your download count against the category average
questions.explain: That's the Sean Ellis test from The Startup Pyramid: above the 40% bar, pour into growth; below it, fix the product first. It complements the retention curve — two independent reads on whether you're in the 'fix the bucket' phase or the 'open the tap' phase.

cards.front: Where do money- and decision-critical events fire?
cards.back: Server-side (posthog-node): signup, activation, subscription, purchase. It's reliable and ad-blocker-immune — and the source of truth for subscription_started is the payment webhook, not the checkout success page.

cards.front: What does autocapture give you, and where does it stop?
cards.back: Pageviews, clicks, form submits, rageclicks, and automatic properties (UTMs, ad-click IDs) with no code — but it records interface events, not meaning. Your funnels need a deliberate set of named custom events.

cards.front: What actually happens when you call posthog.identify?
cards.back: The browser's anonymous person merges into the identified one, so pre- and post-signup activity become one timeline. Merges are effectively permanent — unstable or shared ids are the expensive mistakes.

cards.front: Why call posthog.reset() on logout?
cards.back: Without it, the next person on a shared machine gets merged into the previous user's profile — and merges don't cleanly undo. It's the pair to identify() that keeps identities whole.

cards.prompt: The single biggest cost decision in the whole setup:
cards.cloze: Set person_profiles to {{"identified_only"}} on day one — anonymous events are roughly {{4× cheaper}} than identified ones, so logged-out traffic bills at the cheap rate until you call {{identify}}.

cards.front: What naming rule most reliably saves an analytics setup?
cards.back: Never build event names dynamically. capture(`page_viewed_${page}`) mints thousands of un-queryable event types; capture("page_viewed", { page_name }) keeps one event with the variable in a property.

cards.prompt: A tracking plan is a short, shared spec of every event:
cards.cloze: Its {{name}}, {{trigger}}, {{source}}, and {{key properties}} — owned, not vibes. Keep it in the repo so a new event and its plan row land in the same PR; eight rows is a real plan for an early product.

cards.front: How do you beat ad blockers eating 10–30% of events?
cards.back: Two fixes together: a reverse proxy on your own subdomain recovers blocked client-side analytics, and server-side events never touch the browser at all. The proxy mitigates analytics; server-side fixes the triggers.

questions.q: Where should subscription_started fire, and from what trigger?
options: Client-side, on the checkout success page
Client-side, when the user clicks 'Subscribe'
Server-side, from the payment provider's webhook after signature verification
Both client and server, so you're covered either way
questions.explain: Success pages get closed early, refreshed (double-firing), and ad-blocked. The webhook is the source of truth: it fires exactly once, server-side, regardless of what the browser did. Firing from both sides double-counts revenue.

questions.q: What does person_profiles: 'identified_only' actually do?
options: Blocks anonymous visitors from being tracked at all
Keeps events anonymous (≈4× cheaper) until you call identify, then builds full person profiles
Hides personal data from the PostHog UI
Only tracks users who accepted the cookie banner
questions.explain: It's a cost lever, not a privacy switch: logged-out traffic stays at the cheap anonymous rate, and profiles are only created once you identify someone. For a typical SaaS it's the single biggest billing decision in the setup.

questions.q: An engineer ships posthog.capture(`export_clicked_${format}`) with format taking 30 values. What's wrong?
options: Nothing — more granular events mean better analytics
It creates 30 un-queryable event types; the format belongs in a property of one export_clicked event
Template literals aren't supported by posthog-js
Client-side capture should never carry variables
questions.explain: Dynamic event names are the classic taxonomy-ruining mistake: you can't build one funnel or trend across 30 spellings. One fixed event name with { format } as a property keeps it queryable and keeps your event dictionary readable.

questions.q: You forgot posthog.reset() on logout. What's the failure mode?
options: Sessions expire early for logged-out users
Events stop being captured after logout
The next person to log in on that browser gets merged into the previous user's profile
The anonymous id is regenerated on every pageview
questions.explain: identify merges the browser's current distinct id into the identified person — and merges are effectively permanent. Without reset(), a shared or second-hand browser welds two humans into one person, and no filter cleanly separates them again.

questions.q: Ad blockers are eating client-side events. Which statement is precise?
options: A reverse proxy makes all your tracking immune to blockers
Server-side capture recovers your client-side analytics too
A proxy mitigates client-side analytics loss; server-side events (your journey triggers) were never blockable in the first place
There's no fix — accept the 10–30% loss
questions.explain: Keep the two mechanisms distinct: the proxy recovers a chunk of browser events by using your own domain, while backend events never touch the user's browser at all — which is why money-critical events and lifecycle triggers belong server-side.

questions.q: A security review flags your phc_ project key sitting visible in the shipped JS bundle. What's the right response?
options: Nothing to fix — the project key can only write events; reading your data needs a separate personal API key, which you do keep secret
Rotate the key immediately and move every capture call server-side
Proxy the key through your backend so it never reaches the browser
Restrict the key to your domain in PostHog settings before re-shipping
questions.explain: The write/read split is by design, not a leak: the phc_ key in your bundle can send events but cannot query anything back. It's the personal API key — the one that reads data — that must never ship client-side.

questions.q: Autocapture is on and busily recording clicks, pageviews, and rageclicks. Why can't it power your activation funnel by itself?
options: Autocapture samples at high volume, so funnels systematically under-count
Autocapture only works on marketing pages, not inside a logged-in product
Autocaptured events can't carry properties, so there's nothing to filter on
It records interface events — 'a button was clicked' — not meaning like 'the user activated'; that signal needs named custom events
questions.explain: Autocapture is the safety net, not the signal: it can't know which click was your aha moment. The mature pattern is autocapture on for discovery plus a small, deliberate set of named events for the funnels you actually report on.

questions.q: PostHog is your only analytics destination, and a teammate proposes routing all events through Segment first 'to do it properly'. What does this chapter advise?
options: Skip the CDP for now — with one destination it adds a hop and a bill for nothing, and it earns its keep only once a second destination exists
Adopt Segment early, because retrofitting a CDP later means re-instrumenting everything
Route through Google Tag Manager instead, since it needs no deploy
Split the difference: Segment for client events, PostHog SDKs for server events
questions.explain: A CDP is worth it when the same events fan out to many destinations under one canonical schema. With PostHog as the sole destination, your own front-end and back-end doing the capturing is the cheaper, simpler default.

questions.q: Months in, you discover three differently-named events all mean 'started checkout'. What's the cheapest fix that keeps your history?
options: Rename the events in code and backfill the old data with a migration
Define an Action in PostHog that groups the three events — actions apply retroactively, with no code to ship
Delete the old events and start over with one clean checkout_started
Build three parallel funnels and sum the numbers in a spreadsheet
questions.explain: Actions are groupings defined inside PostHog that apply to events you already captured — the escape hatch for the inevitable taxonomy miss. It's why a good taxonomy matters but a perfect one isn't worth blocking on.

questions.q: Your team wants to start A/B testing, but the goal metric project_created currently fires from two places under three names. What's the right order of operations?
options: Run the experiment anyway — instrumentation noise averages out across variants
Use a before/after comparison instead, since it doesn't depend on event quality
Switch the goal metric to pageviews, which autocapture already tracks cleanly
Clean the instrumentation first — experiments measure a variant against an event, so dirty events make results measure your tracking, not your product
questions.explain: The dependency runs one direction: instrument first, test second. And before/after comparisons are no refuge — they feel like measurement but can't separate your change from the newsletter that went out the same day.

cards.prompt: The daily four:
cards.cloze: Of PostHog's seven insight types, the four you'll read daily are {{Trends}}, {{Funnels}}, {{Retention}}, and {{Lifecycle}}.

cards.front: What is the Trends failure mode?
cards.back: Counting events when you mean people: ten pageviews from one user and one pageview from ten users look identical until you switch the series to unique users.

cards.front: Which two funnel settings do people commonly miss?
cards.back: The conversion window (a day-15 convert in a 7-day window counts as dropped) and exclusion steps. If conversion looks impossibly low, check User Paths — your steps may not match the order users follow.

cards.front: What does a big Dormant band under modest New growth mean?
cards.back: Churn-and-replace, not growth — the same users leaving and being replaced. It's the leaky bucket drawn for you; acquisition can wait, retention (Chapters 5–6) is the work.

cards.prompt: The three retention-curve shapes:
cards.cloze: A curve that {{slides to zero}} means no PMF, one that {{declines then flattens above zero}} is the product-market-fit signal, and the rare one that {{ticks back up}} means resurrection exceeds churn — the flattening level is roughly your ceiling without intervention.

cards.front: Why do small cohorts lie?
cards.back: In a 12-user cohort a 40% to 25% swing is three people — not roadmap material. The signal worth acting on is the trend across several cohorts, not one noisy row.

cards.front: How do you keep session replay cheap and diagnostic?
cards.back: Arrive from a chart — a funnel drop-off, error, or rage-click filter — so every replay is pre-selected to show a problem. What you're looking for is the moment of hesitation.

cards.front: Why know your funnel baseline by heart?
cards.back: So a change registers as a change, not a guess (say, 62% reach step 2, 31% activate). The fixed list read the same way every morning is what makes a deviation register instantly.

questions.q: Your lifecycle chart shows New users steady, but the Dormant band is growing every week. What is it telling you?
options: Acquisition is failing — spend more on ads
Growth is churn-and-replace: you're refilling a leaking bucket, and the priority is retention (Chapters 5–6)
The chart needs a longer date range
Users are switching devices, splitting their identity
questions.explain: Lifecycle decomposes 'active users' into New, Returning, Resurrecting, and Dormant. Steady New with swelling Dormant means the same users churn and get replaced — the leaky bucket drawn for you. More acquisition makes that worse, not better.

questions.q: A retention curve declines for four weeks and then flattens at 30%. The honest reading is:
options: The product is losing 70% of users — it's failing
Roughly three in ten users stick long-term — a real sign of product pull, and the flattening level is the ceiling lifecycle messaging works to lift
Retention is 30% so the product has reached its potential
The curve must reach zero eventually; the flattening is a data artifact
questions.explain: Shape first, height second: any flattening above zero means some cohort found durable value — the core PMF signal. The level it flattens at is your ceiling without intervention, which is exactly what Chapters 5–6 work on raising.

questions.q: Your activation funnel shows an impossibly low conversion. Before redesigning onboarding, what should you check?
options: Whether the conversion window is shorter than the time users actually take, and whether the steps match the order users really follow (User Paths)
Whether the dashboard's date filter is set to 'all time'
Whether autocapture is enabled
Whether the funnel uses unique users instead of events
questions.explain: The two silent funnel-killers are a conversion window that expires before real users convert, and steps in an order users don't follow. Both make a healthy product look broken. Verify the instrument before treating the reading.

questions.q: What's the right way to use session replay in the daily ritual?
options: Browse recent replays for 15 minutes to get a feel for users
Watch every new user's first session
Arrive from a chart — a funnel drop-off, error, or rage-click filter — so each replay is pre-selected to show a known problem
Only watch replays when a customer complains
questions.explain: Cold-browsing replays is expensive and anecdotal. Chart-first selection means the quantitative layer finds where the leak is and replay supplies the why — five minutes of watching becomes diagnosis instead of tourism.

questions.q: An alert has fired every morning for two weeks and nobody has done anything about it. The correct move is:
options: Leave it — better safe than sorry
Act on it or re-threshold it; an alert that's ignorable trains the team to ignore the one that matters
Move it from Slack to email
Add three more alerts so patterns are easier to spot
questions.explain: Alert fatigue is the failure mode of §4.5: every fired alert gets acted on or re-tuned, no third option. Few loud alerts that always mean something beat many quiet ones that never do.

questions.q: Your pageviews Trend doubled overnight, but signups and activation are flat. Before celebrating, what should you check first?
options: Whether a new marketing channel just kicked in
Whether the dashboard date filter got changed
Whether autocapture is double-counting pageviews
Whether the series counts events instead of unique users — ten pageviews from one user look identical to one pageview from ten
questions.explain: The Trends failure mode is counting events when you mean people. Switching the series to unique users tells you whether more humans actually showed up or the same ones just clicked more.

questions.q: A weekly retention cohort of 12 users drops from 40% to 25%, and a teammate wants to re-plan the roadmap. The right response is:
options: That swing is about three people — wait for a trend across several cohorts before concluding anything
Act now: a 15-point drop is a crisis at any cohort size
Switch the grid to unbounded retention, which is more forgiving
Widen the return-event definition so more users count as retained
questions.explain: Small cohorts lie: in a 12-user cohort each person is worth over eight points, so 40% to 25% is three people. The signal worth acting on is the trend across several cohorts, not one noisy row.

questions.q: Your activation funnel reads 24% against the 31% baseline you know by heart. Following the chapter's Monday read, what comes next?
options: Rewrite the onboarding copy on the worst step
Increase acquisition spend to compensate for lost activations
Check Error Tracking for new exceptions, then watch replays of the drop-off — an error spike often explains an activation dip
Wait a few weeks to see whether the number recovers on its own
questions.explain: In the worked example, the 31%-to-24% dip traced to a new validation exception, not bad copy — errors get checked before blaming onboarding. Knowing the baseline by heart is what made the deviation register at all.

questions.q: You want to know what separates your power users — the ones who fire the core action five days a week — from everyone else. Which insight fits?
options: Retention — it shows whether users came back
Stickiness — it measures how many times users did something in a period, and what the deep-usage group shares is an activation-bar candidate
User Paths — it maps every route through the product
Funnels — the biggest drop is your biggest leak
questions.explain: Retention asks whether users returned; Stickiness asks how often. The chapter's example: if five-day-a-week users all created three projects in week one, 'three projects' becomes an activation bar worth testing with Chapter 2's method.

questions.q: A teammate proposes a dedicated dashboard per feature — forty boards, so nothing gets missed. The chapter's position is:
options: Good idea — more visibility always beats less
Fine, as long as each board has a named owner
Split the difference with one dashboard per team
Dashboard sprawl means none is canonical — one pinned board everyone reads the same way beats a wall of charts nobody trusts
questions.explain: A team with forty dashboards has none, because nobody knows which one is canonical. The daily read works precisely because it's one fixed list read against a known baseline — sprawl dissolves that baseline.

cards.front: What is lifecycle messaging?
cards.back: The right message, triggered by the right behaviour, at the right time — the operational layer that moves users between AARRR stages, and the cheapest retention lever because the users are already yours.

cards.prompt: The Reforge retention model:
cards.cloze: Retention is an {{output}}, not a stage you push on directly — its inputs are {{activation}}, {{engagement}}, and {{resurrection}}, and lifecycle messaging is how you move each one.

cards.front: Why build welcome + onboarding first?
cards.back: Peak intent — welcome opens commonly run 35–50% — the signup-to-activation gap is the biggest leak, and activated users retain roughly 2× better. Dunning comes next if you charge, then win-back.

cards.front: Why is dunning the highest dollar-ROI journey?
cards.back: Involuntary churn (failed cards, expirations) is 20–40% of SaaS churn, and these users want to keep paying — you're removing friction, not persuading. Exit the instant a payment succeeds.

cards.prompt: Triggered vs batch, the benchmark:
cards.cloze: Automated flows are roughly {{5% of sends}} but about {{40% of email revenue}} (directional) — a batch send's relevance is the list's average, while a triggered send's is pinned to a moment of intent.

cards.front: What role may time play in a trigger?
cards.back: Only as a window on behaviour — 'not done in 24–72h' — never as the reason to send. A trigger column filling with durations is the time-based drip quietly creeping back in.

cards.front: What's the honest signal that you're over-messaging?
cards.back: Engagement per send drops as you add messages — opens, clicks, or revenue-per-email falling with volume is the program telling you it's reached the useful maximum.

cards.front: What is frequency capping, and why is it load-bearing?
cards.back: A ceiling on total messages per user across all journeys and channels. It prevents the pile-up where several individually-justified journeys fire in the same bad week — the pattern that gets you marked as spam.

questions.q: Why does the build order start with welcome + onboarding rather than win-back or referral?
options: Welcome emails are the easiest to write
New users are at peak intent, and the signup→activation gap is the biggest leak in the bucket
Win-back requires a paid email tool
Referral programs are illegal in some jurisdictions
questions.explain: Engagement is never higher than right after signup, and most early churn happens before activation — so the first journey targets the widest hole at the moment of highest attention. Everything else compounds off users who activated.

questions.q: A subscription user's card expires and their payment fails. Which is true?
options: This is voluntary churn and should get a win-back offer
This is involuntary churn — commonly 20–40% of SaaS churn — and a dunning sequence recovers it by removing friction, not persuading
Nothing can be done until the user notices
The correct response is a discount
questions.explain: Dunning is the highest dollar-ROI journey for a paid product precisely because the user already wants to pay. Retries plus a one-click card-update link recover revenue you'd otherwise silently lose.

questions.q: What's the honest signal that you're over-messaging?
options: Any unsubscribes at all
Sending more than one email per week
Engagement per send drops as you add messages
Your open rate is below 50%
questions.explain: Cadence has no universal right answer — the dial is calibrated per product by watching whether each added touch still earns engagement. Falling opens/clicks/revenue-per-email as volume rises is the stop signal.

questions.q: Since 2024, which of these is a hard requirement for bulk senders to reach Gmail/Yahoo inboxes?
options: A minimum open rate of 20%
SPF, DKIM and DMARC authentication plus one-click unsubscribe, with spam complaints under 0.3%
Sending only on weekdays
Using a dedicated IP address
questions.explain: Google and Yahoo turned the old soft best-practices into enforced rules: authenticate your domain, honour one-click unsubscribe within two days, and keep complaint rates under 0.3% — or your mail simply doesn't land.

questions.q: Your welcome journey shows a 45% open rate and the upgrade rate of messaged users is 18%. What do you still not know?
options: Whether the subject lines are good
Whether the emails render on mobile
Whether the journey caused any of those upgrades — you need a holdout comparison to measure incremental lift
Whether users read to the end
questions.explain: Opens and even conversions among messaged users can't distinguish 'the email worked' from 'these users would have upgraded anyway.' Only the difference against a randomly held-out group is causal — that's the incrementality discipline.

questions.q: A user's card fails the same week they go dormant and approach a plan limit — and three separate journeys each send twice. Every message was individually justified. What practice prevents this?
options: Better subject lines so the messages feel less repetitive
Pausing all journeys whenever a payment fails
Merging everything into one mega-journey
A frequency cap — a ceiling on total messages per user across all journeys and channels
questions.explain: Each journey can be correct in isolation and still produce a spam-worthy pile-up in combination — that's exactly the failure frequency capping exists for. It becomes load-bearing the moment you run more than one journey.

questions.q: Reviewing your journey table, you notice the trigger column filling up with entries like 'it's been 7 days since email #2'. What does the chapter say about this?
options: Time should appear only as a window on behaviour ('not done in 24–72h'), never as the reason to send — this is the drip you were escaping, creeping back in
It's fine, since the welcome cadence is every 2–3 days anyway
Time-based triggers are preferable because they're easier to test
It's fine as long as open rates stay healthy
questions.explain: The core argument for triggered-beats-batch is because relevance is pinned to a behavioural moment. When durations replace behaviours as the reason to send, you've rebuilt the rigid time-based drip the chapter says to drop.

questions.q: You're writing the first touch of a win-back for users of a tax product who've been idle 30 days. What's the chapter-aligned move?
options: Send 'we miss you' with a discount — incentives drive the highest reactivation
Treat 30 days idle as churned and send a final-notice offer immediately
Check your own usage interval first — 30 days idle in a tax product may be normal — and lead with what changed since they left
Skip win-back entirely; reactivation is more expensive than acquiring new users
questions.explain: Dormancy thresholds come from your retention curve, not a template — 30 days idle in a daily-use product is churn, in a tax product it's Tuesday. And 'we miss you' answers no question the user has; 'here's what's new' does, with incentives held for last.

questions.q: A journey enrolls about 200 users a month. You set a 5% holdout, and after one week the messaged group looks clearly ahead, so you call it a win. What went wrong?
options: The holdout is too small for the volume (use 20–50% until it grows), and peeking early biases the read — the read date should be decided in advance
Nothing — a week is enough when the lift looks that large
You should have compared against last month's cohort instead of a holdout
5% is the right size, but the test needed to run a full quarter regardless of volume
questions.explain: A 5% holdout of 200 users is ~10 people — far too few to detect anything — and stopping the moment numbers look good is the peeking mistake the holdouts piece warns about. Both errors make the 'lift' you report indistinguishable from noise.

questions.q: Which of these messages passes the chapter's test for real personalisation — 'could this exact message have been sent to a user in a different state?'
options: 'Hi {first name}, here are five tips for getting started'
A weekly product digest sent to the whole list
'Hi {first name}, welcome to the community!'
'You created 3 projects but haven't invited a teammate yet'
questions.explain: Real personalisation branches on what the user did or didn't do — a message tied to their actual product state couldn't be sent to anyone else. First-name merge fields and generic tips fail the test, and fake personalisation quietly teaches users your emails aren't worth opening.

flashcards:cards-hogsend.title: Key ideas — the behavioural nudge series

cards.prompt: The shape of a behavioural nudge series:
cards.cloze: The loop is {{wait a beat}}, {{check whether the desired action happened}}, then {{send the next nudge only if it didn't}} — so an active user is left alone and a stuck user gets help.

cards.front: Why does 'send only if needed' beat a fixed drip?
cards.back: A drip fires every message to everyone on a timer regardless of behaviour; the series only reaches users who actually stalled. That's more relevant to them and more respectful of their attention — so it earns the next open instead of burning it.

cards.prompt: What escalation actually means:
cards.cloze: Escalation isn't {{louder}} — it's {{later}}: each nudge targets the {{next blocker in the activation path}}, assuming the previous step is done, because the behavioural gate guarantees it.

cards.front: When should a nudge series stop?
cards.back: The instant the user activates — the goal is met, so every further nudge is noise that contradicts their behaviour. Failing that, cap it at roughly three to five touches; a series with no exit becomes nagging and trains people to unsubscribe.

cards.prompt: The relevance test for every touch:
cards.cloze: A nudge like 'you haven't tried X' is {{only a good email if it's true}} — the whole series depends on gating each send on {{the user's real behaviour}}, never on a guess.

cards.front: What's the difference between a program that waits and one that listens?
cards.back: A timer fires regardless of what the user did; a behavioural series races the user's own actions against the wait and branches on what actually happened. One counts down, the other pays attention.

cards.front: Why suppress activated users from the rest of the series?
cards.back: Once someone reaches the aha moment the series has done its job. Continuing to nudge them contradicts their behaviour and reads as 'this company isn't watching' — the fastest way to lose the trust the welcome earned.

cards.prompt: How to prove the series caused the lift:
cards.cloze: Hold back a {{random slice}} of stalled users from the nudges and compare {{activation rate}} against the nudged cohort — the gap is the {{lift the series actually caused}}, not the correlation you'd get from 'users who opened the emails'.

calc:ch6-dunning-recovery.title: What dunning is worth to you

questions.q: You have time to build exactly one lifecycle email this week. For almost every product, which one has the highest return?
options: A win-back for dormant users — reactivation is the most neglected stage
The welcome + activation nudge — the signup-to-activation gap is nearly always the biggest leak
A referral ask — it's the cheapest acquisition there is
A monthly newsletter — it keeps everyone warm
questions.explain: Emails are built in order of expected impact, and the biggest leak for almost every product sits between signup and activation. Fixing that saves users you already paid to acquire, which is why it comes before everything else.

questions.q: A free user hasn't done the core action yet, and your instinct is to send them an 'upgrade to paid' email. The chapter's read:
options: Send it — more upgrade prompts lift conversion
Don't — conversion is earned by activation; asking someone who hasn't felt the value to pay just earns an unsubscribe
Send it, but offer a discount to compensate
Send it only if they opened the welcome email
questions.explain: The most common mistake in the funnel is asking for money before the user reached the aha moment. Conversion emails work on activated users; on unactivated ones they push on a door that never opened.

questions.q: Why is dunning (failed-payment recovery) usually the fastest revenue win in the course?
options: It's the easiest email to design
It recovers involuntary churn — customers who already chose to pay and just had a card fail — so you're saving revenue that's already yours
It lets you raise prices without complaints
It brings back users who cancelled on purpose
questions.explain: A meaningful share of churn is involuntary — expired cards, insufficient funds. Those customers wanted to keep paying, so a retry-plus-reminder sequence recovers real revenue for almost no acquisition cost.

questions.q: When should the referral ask fire?
options: On signup, while enthusiasm is highest
On a fixed monthly schedule to everyone
At a moment of peak satisfaction — just after the aha or a great outcome — and only to still-active users
When a user is about to churn, as a last-ditch effort
questions.explain: People refer when they've just felt the value, not before they've felt it and not on your calendar. Asking dormant or unhappy users wastes the ask and can annoy them; timing it to a real win is the whole tactic.

questions.q: You're running a dozen journeys and a single user is eligible for several at once. The single most important guardrail is:
options: A global frequency cap enforced across all journeys, so no combination can pile on
Sending everything at 9am so it arrives together
Turning off every journey but the most important one
Asking users to pick which emails they want
questions.explain: Overlapping journeys are how a helpful program becomes spam. A cap enforced across every journey — not per-journey — is what protects the user from your enthusiasm and your deliverability from complaints.

questions.q: Two journeys could fire for the same user at the same moment: a dunning email and a new-feature nudge. Which should win, and on what principle?
options: The feature nudge — new features drive engagement
Whichever was scheduled first
Dunning — the rule of thumb is money and trust (payments, security, receipts) outrank promotional nudges
Neither — hold both until tomorrow
questions.explain: When the frequency cap forces a choice, you need a priority order decided up front. Revenue-critical and trust-critical messages outrank promotion, so the failed-payment email sends and the feature nudge waits.

questions.q: Your welcome journey's open rate is 61% and you want to report the lifecycle program as a win. What actually proves the program's worth?
options: The open rate, since it's well above benchmark
The total number of emails sent this month
A global holdout — a small random slice held out of all lifecycle email — compared to everyone else on retention and revenue
The number of journeys live
questions.explain: Opens measure sending, not lift. A permanent program holdout is the only thing that shows what the whole lifecycle system is actually worth — the number your monthly growth review and a skeptical cofounder both ask for.

questions.q: A feedback email comes back with a detractor score and an angry comment. In a lifecycle system, what should happen next?
options: Log it to a dashboard and move on
It triggers a save flow — a real reply or a 'what went wrong' journey — rather than vanishing into silence
Immediately send a discount code
Remove them from all future emails
questions.explain: The feedback email isn't the end of a journey; it's a fork. A detractor should trigger a save/reply flow and a promoter a referral or review ask — routing the answer is what turns a survey into a system.

questions.q: It's a trial-ending sequence. A teammate suggests a countdown timer that resets on every page refresh to boost urgency. The chapter's view:
options: Do it — manufactured urgency reliably lifts conversion
Don't — fake scarcity converts a few once and costs trust with everyone who notices; honest urgency ('your trial ends Friday, here's what changes') converts about as well without spending credibility
Do it, but only for users who haven't activated
Only use it in the final email, not the whole sequence
questions.explain: Real deadlines are honest urgency; a timer that resets is a trick. It works once and quietly erodes the trust the rest of your lifecycle program depends on — a bad trade.

questions.q: What makes a triggered, per-user lifecycle email beat a batch blast to your whole list?
options: It's cheaper to send
It reaches the right person at the moment their behaviour makes the message relevant — a receipt-not-brochure email doing one job, rather than the same mail to everyone regardless of where they are
It always has higher open rates
It doesn't need an unsubscribe link
questions.explain: The whole point of a lifecycle system is relevance: the email fires off what the user actually did, so it lands when it's useful. A batch blast ignores where each person is in the funnel, which is why it's the thing this course is built to replace.

cards.front: What is the law of shitty clickthroughs?
cards.back: Every channel's performance degrades as it saturates — banner ads fell from 78% CTR in 1994 to ~0.05%. The counter is capturing what the channel brings into something you own, not channel-hopping.

cards.front: Should a dev tool lead with paid social?
cards.back: Mostly no — developers block ads at the highest rate and discover tools through content, GitHub, HN, docs, and each other. Treat paid as fuel for the loop and point most energy at owned channels (Chapter 8).

cards.front: How do you compute CAC honestly?
cards.back: Fully loaded spend — ads plus the content, tools, and people behind them — divided by paying customers, not signups. Per-channel CAC is the operating metric; blended CAC can hide an unprofitable channel.

cards.prompt: The LTV:CAC maths:
cards.cloze: LTV is the {{gross-margin}} contribution over a customer's lifetime — roughly monthly margin ÷ {{monthly churn}}, never revenue — and the folk benchmark is LTV:CAC of at least {{3}} with payback (months of margin to recover CAC) inside {{about 12 months}}.

cards.front: What's the most consequential choice in a Meta campaign?
cards.back: The objective — it tells the delivery algorithm which kind of person to go find. Traffic buys visitors who click; Sales buys visitors who act. They are different people.

cards.prompt: The 50-conversions rule:
cards.cloze: Meta's algorithm needs roughly {{50 conversions}} per ad set per {{week}} to exit the {{learning phase}} — optimise the deepest event you can feed at that volume, pass deeper conversions back server-side, and change things weekly, since {{edits reset learning}}.

cards.front: Why is creative now the targeting?
cards.back: With broad and Advantage+ audiences the ad itself selects the audience — only developers watch a video about deploy previews. Winners fatigue in weeks, and the first three seconds are where qualification happens.

cards.front: What makes a good lookalike seed?
cards.back: Seed quality dominates the result: your best customers — recent purchasers, high-LTV — beat 'everyone who ever signed up.' A 1% lookalike is the tightest match; widening to 3–10% trades match for scale.

questions.q: You're a dev tool with $2k/month to spend on growth. Based on this chapter, the best default allocation is:
options: All of it on Meta ads with broad targeting
Mostly content, docs, and community — with paid (if any) as a small, honestly-measured test
All of it on LinkedIn, since developers are professionals
Split evenly across every channel to A/B test them
questions.explain: Developers block ads, distrust interruption, and discover tools through content, GitHub, HN, and each other. Paid social is weak for dev tools — the chapter's advice is to learn how paid works but put the energy into owned channels (Chapter 8).

questions.q: Your blended CAC looks healthy, but you spend heavily on paid. What's the trap?
options: Blended CAC double-counts organic users
Blended averages free organic acquisition into the paid number — a per-channel CAC could reveal paid is unprofitable on its own
CAC should never include ad spend
There is no trap; blended CAC is the standard
questions.explain: Blended CAC is a board metric; per-channel CAC is the operating metric. Paid-social cohorts also tend to retain worse than organic, so segment LTV by source too before declaring a channel profitable.

questions.q: You get ~10 purchases a week but optimised your Meta ad set for Purchase anyway. What happens?
options: Meta refunds under-delivered budget
The ad set stays starved in the learning phase and never finds the right people — optimise a higher-funnel event and send the purchase back via CAPI instead
Nothing — the algorithm works the same at any volume
Meta automatically switches your objective to Traffic
questions.explain: The delivery algorithm needs roughly 50 conversions per ad set per week to learn. Below that, optimise for an event you CAN feed at volume (signup, lead) and pass the deeper conversion server-side so the system still learns from it.

questions.q: You've set up CAPI alongside the Pixel and conversions doubled overnight. Most likely explanation?
options: CAPI recovered conversions ad blockers were hiding
The campaign hit its learning-phase exit
You're double-counting: Pixel and CAPI events need the same event_id so Meta can deduplicate
EMQ improved, so Meta found more buyers
questions.explain: Recovered conversions from blockers/ITP are real but incremental (think a fifth, not a doubling). A sudden 2× is the classic missing-event_id dedup failure — and it corrupts the ROAS you built the setup to measure.

questions.q: Which of these does server-side tracking genuinely fix?
options: The need for user consent when sharing PII with ad platforms
Per-user iOS app-install attribution
Sending high-value back-office conversions (trial→paid, qualified-lead) that the browser never sees
Low-quality events that carry no email, phone, or external_id
questions.explain: Back-office conversions are CAPI's biggest genuine win — your most valuable events, invisible to any pixel. It is NOT a consent loophole (hashed email is pseudonymous, not anonymous), doesn't restore iOS install attribution (SKAdNetwork territory), and can't match events that carry no identifiers.

questions.q: The channel that reliably brought you users last year keeps getting more expensive per acquisition. Per the law of shitty clickthroughs, the right response is:
options: Keep working it while capturing what it brings into channels you own — decay is normal and expected
Hop to the newest channel immediately, since a decaying channel never recovers
Double the budget to buy back the lost efficiency
Pause all acquisition until the channel's performance returns
questions.explain: Andrew Chen's observation is that every channel degrades as it saturates — banner ads went from 78% CTR in 1994 to ~0.05%. The counter isn't channel-hopping; it's converting the channel's traffic into an owned audience (Chapter 8) while the channel still works.

questions.q: You ran a Traffic campaign, got thousands of cheap clicks, and almost no signups. What went wrong?
options: Your landing page must be broken
Meta throttled conversions because the ad account is new
Traffic campaigns can't convert without a Pixel installed
Traffic optimises for people who click, not people who act — a Sales or Leads objective finds different people
questions.explain: The campaign objective tells Meta's delivery algorithm which kind of person to go find. Traffic buys visitors who clicked; Sales buys visitors who act — they are different people, and cheap clicks that never convert is the classic beginner trap.

questions.q: Your ad set has been live four days and performance looks shaky, so you're tempted to tweak the budget and swap creative daily until it improves. The chapter's advice?
options: Hold off — significant edits reset the learning phase, so daily tweaks keep the ad set permanently learning and permanently expensive
Edit daily — fresh creative always beats algorithmic stability
Split into five smaller ad sets so each one can learn independently
Switch the objective to Traffic, which exits learning faster
questions.explain: Every significant change can send the ad set back to school, so the check-daily-worry-edit pattern is permanently expensive; weekly changes on Chapter 9's cadence give the system time to settle. And five ad sets splitting 10 conversions a week will all starve — fewer, bigger ad sets learn faster.

questions.q: You're building your first lookalike audience. Which seed does the chapter say will perform best?
options: Everyone who ever signed up — the biggest seed gives Meta the most data
A 10% lookalike of all site visitors, for maximum scale
Your best customers — recent purchasers or high-LTV users — even though the list is smaller
A hand-built interest stack matching your ideal demographics
questions.explain: Seed quality dominates the result: Meta finds people resembling the seed, so your best customers make a far better seed than 'everyone who ever signed up.' A 1% lookalike is the tightest match; widening to 3–10% trades match quality for scale.

questions.q: A vendor pitches you server-side CAPI with SHA-256-hashed emails as a way to skip consent, since the data is 'anonymised.' What's wrong with that?
options: Nothing — hashing is a recognised anonymisation technique
Consent only applies to the browser Pixel, not server events
It's only a concern for companies based in Germany
Hashed emails are pseudonymous, not anonymous — same input, same hash — and since January 2025 Meta itself requires explicit consent before you send PII via CAPI
questions.explain: Server-side is not a consent loophole: hashing does not make data anonymous in law, you are the data controller when you share contact data, and a German regulator has ruled unconsented list uploads unlawful. The compliant machine starts with opt-in consent at the moment you capture the email.

cards.front: What's the difference between an owned audience and a rented one?
cards.back: Ad audiences and social followings are rented: you pay, or the algorithm taxes, every reach. A verified email or linked Discord/Telegram is owned — no algorithm tax, near-free reactivation, and portable.

cards.front: How should you treat "acquiring costs 5–25× more than retaining"?
cards.back: As direction, not decimals — the multiples are loosely-sourced folklore. The load-bearing mechanism is real: an ad spend that captures a contact into an owned channel turns a rented impression into a reusable asset.

cards.prompt: The capture mechanic:
cards.cloze: "Link your account or verify your email → get {{a perk}}" — the perk is {{the conversion event}}, and the conversion is {{the identity capture}}.

cards.front: How valuable should the linking perk be?
cards.back: Worth the link and no more — over-rewarding harvests perk-hunters who churn once the reward lands. The best perks are things a genuine user wanted anyway: community access, product credits, early features.

cards.front: Why is the typed email never trusted in the /link flow?
cards.back: The delivered-and-clicked email IS the proof of inbox ownership, making the address real and consented. An unverified typed address is worthless as an ad-audience input — possibly unlawful to upload.

cards.front: What is the identity graph?
cards.back: Every link event writes an edge: email ↔ Discord ↔ Telegram ↔ wallet ↔ anon id ↔ PostHog person. Enough edges give one profile across devices and channels: retroactive stitching, cheaper reactivation, better ads.

cards.prompt: The community playbook — the part the tooling can't do:
cards.cloze: Start with {{a mission}}, not a server; {{fewer channels}} than feels right (three active beat twenty empty); recurring {{rituals}}; and a {{hand-recruited}} first 100 members, until conversation self-sustains.

cards.front: What do referral programs deliver when done right — and what do they need to work?
cards.back: Done right, referrals drive 15–50% of new-user growth, yet most programs see under 10% of users ever refer (Lenny Rachitsky's survey). And a program only works if you can attribute it — exactly what the owned identity graph provides.

questions.q: Your product has 40,000 followers on X and a 900-person verified-email list. Which is the owned audience, and why?
options: The X following — it's 44× bigger
Both, equally — they're both audiences you built
The email list — the platform algorithm decides which followers see a post, while the list is reachable directly and portable
Neither — only a Discord server counts as owned
questions.explain: A social following is rented reach with a subtler landlord: the feed algorithm taxes every post, and the platform can change the deal at any time. A verified list is directly reachable, exportable, and — per 1,000 True Fans — a few thousand engaged reachable people beat a large rented count.

questions.q: Why does the /link flow email a confirmation link instead of trusting the address the user types?
options: To comply with Discord's API terms
Because the clicked email is the proof of inbox ownership — an unverified address is worthless as an owned contact and possibly unlawful to upload as an ad audience
To slow down the flow and reduce server load
Because Discord hides members' real emails
questions.explain: The delivered-and-clicked email IS the verification. It makes the captured email real (deliverable), consented (they acted on it), and therefore usable — both for lifecycle messaging and as a legal, high-quality custom-audience input for Chapter 7.

questions.q: You launch a referral program offering $25 cash per signup. Six weeks later invites are up but referred-cohort retention is terrible. What happened?
options: The reward was too small to attract serious users
The incentive did the selecting: cash-per-signup attracts perk-hunters and farmers, not people who want the product — reward verified activation milestones with product-aligned value instead
Referral programs simply don't work for most products
The attribution is broken, so the cohort data is wrong
questions.explain: This is Andrew Chen's 'worst users' pattern: heavily-incentivised channels select for people who want the incentive. The fixes: reward verified behaviour (activation, not signup), align the reward to product value like Dropbox's storage, and judge the program on retained users, not invites.

questions.q: A friend shares a referral link; the recipient clicks it on their phone, then signs up on a laptop three weeks later. How does the owned identity graph credit the referrer when cookie-based tracking can't?
options: It fingerprints the user's devices
It stores a longer-lived third-party cookie
