The anonymous visit recorded the referral edge; when the visitor later identifies (email verify, Discord link), the graph resolves them to one person and back-credits the referrer
It asks the user who referred them at signup
questions.explain: First-party referral attribution rides the graph: the anonymous id from the phone click and the identified laptop signup both stitch to the same person once identity lands, so the referrer ↔ referee edge survives the cross-device gap that kills cookie attribution.

questions.q: You stood up a Discord with 22 channels and a verification gate; 300 people joined via the perk and the server is silent. The chapter's diagnosis:
options: The perk wasn't valuable enough
You need a bigger moderation team
You built the capture mechanic but not the community: too many channels dilute conversation, there's no mission or ritual, and the first hundred members were never hand-recruited into talking
Discord is the wrong platform — move to Slack
questions.explain: Linking captures identity; it doesn't create conversation. The playbook: a mission people join, three channels instead of twenty (density is the product), a weekly ritual, and unscalable personal recruiting until conversation self-sustains.

questions.q: A teammate's slide cites 'acquiring costs 5–25× more than retaining' as a precise, sourced fact. How does the chapter say to treat numbers like these?
options: As direction, not decimals — the multiples are loosely-sourced folklore, but the load-bearing mechanism is real: an ad spend that captures an owned contact turns a rented impression into a reusable asset
As reliable benchmarks — they're cited across the industry for a reason
As irrelevant — cost comparisons don't apply to owned channels
As underestimates — owned channels are effectively free to build
questions.explain: The chapter flags these multiples as directional folklore with loose sourcing. What it stakes the argument on is the mechanism: pay once to capture the contact, then reach them as often as you like for free — which holds whatever the true multiple is.

questions.q: To boost Discord linking, you raise the perk to $100 of product credit. Link conversions triple. What does the chapter predict comes next?
options: Sustainable growth — a stronger perk compounds the flywheel
Discord will flag the server for incentivised invites
Nothing changes — perk size only affects the conversion rate
A wave of perk-hunters who churn the moment the reward lands — the perk should be worth the link and no more, ideally something a genuine user wanted anyway
questions.explain: Over-rewarding means the incentive does the selecting — the same failure mode as referral bounties in §8.7. The best perks (community access, credits a real user would spend, early features) convert the people who wanted the product anyway.

questions.q: Telegram members already have verified phone numbers, so why does the Telegram /link flow still email a single-use confirmation link?
options: Telegram's bot API requires email verification before a bot can DM

Telegram identity is phone-based with no native email field — the deep-link plus email-confirm flow is the same proof-by-clicking pattern as Discord, and it's what binds telegram ↔ email into the graph
The phone number is captured as the owned contact; the email is just a backup
To rate-limit how often members can run /link
questions.explain: The asset being captured is the verified email edge, not the phone number. Clicking the emailed link proves inbox ownership and stitches the analytics identity from the client — the same never-trust-the-typed-address standard the Discord flow sets.

questions.q: You want a simple give-get credit referral live this week, on one channel, web only. Per the chapter's build-vs-buy split, what's the right first move?
options: A hosted referral tool like Rewardful or GrowSurf — buy when link-based attribution is enough; build on your own graph when referrals go cross-device, live in your community, or attract fraud
Build it on your identity graph from day one — hosted tools are a dead end
PartnerStack — it's the standard for user-to-user referral programs
Skip referrals entirely — under 10% of users ever refer, so the channel rarely pays
questions.explain: The chapter's honest split: hosted SaaS is the right first move for a simple single-channel cash or credit program, while the owned-graph build wins when attribution must survive the phone-click → laptop-signup gap, rewards live in the community, or anti-fraud needs verified identity. PartnerStack is affiliate/partner-shaped, and the under-10% figure describes median programs, not the ceiling.

questions.q: A well-funded competitor ships a feature-for-feature clone of your product in a single quarter. Why does the chapter argue you can still be defensible?
options: Feature velocity — you can out-ship them
Brand loyalty means users ignore clones
You can't — feature parity means the moat is gone
The consented identity graph is a hidden network: it has to be earned edge by edge, it's reachable by you and no competitor, and every new link makes the next turn of the loop cheaper
questions.explain: This is the Dixon/a16z layer: the tool earns the user, the network keeps them — and an identity graph is a network effect that doesn't look like one. Features can be copied in a quarter; a dense, consented graph is accumulated one verified link at a time.

cards.prompt: The ordering agreed on Day 0 — the most valuable decision in the course:
cards.cloze: {{Measure}} → {{keep}} → {{grow}}: acquisition goes last because it {{wastes the most money when the bucket leaks}} and pays back the most when it doesn't.

cards.prompt: The division of labour that keeps the system simple:
cards.cloze: PostHog {{detects}} (events, funnels, retention). Hogsend {{acts}} (lifecycle journeys across email, Discord, Telegram). You {{decide}}.

cards.front: What's the daily job in the operating cadence?
cards.back: Two minutes of noticing, not analysing: glance at the Slack alerts, the activation funnel, and the active-users trend — dig only if an alert fired. A glance over five minutes means the alerts need tuning.

cards.front: What does the weekly growth review look like?
cards.back: 30 minutes, fixed agenda: metrics read, journeys and spend (kill-or-scale, out loud), experiments. It reviews learnings and decisions, not status — anomalies get an owner, not live group debugging.

cards.front: Which questions are monthly, not weekly?
cards.back: Is activation still the best predictor of retention; incremental lift from the global holdout; blended CAC and the referral loop's contribution; and a prune of no-lift journeys, unqueried events, dead ad sets.

cards.prompt: The growth-hypothesis format — every backlog idea enters this way, ICE-scored (impact, confidence, ease, 1–10):
cards.cloze: If we {{make this specific change}}, {{this named metric}} will move {{in this direction}}, because {{this reasoning}}.

cards.front: What's Sean Ellis's high-tempo testing argument?
cards.back: The compounding asset is your velocity of tests — learning compounds even when individual tests fail. For a startup, one experiment a week, every week, beats a burst of ten followed by a quarter of nothing.

cards.front: What are the three honest rules of an experimentation program?
cards.back: Not everything deserves an experiment (obvious, reversible, or too-small changes just ship); sample size decides what you can learn — big swings beat button colours at startup traffic; write the losers down too.

questions.q: What's the daily job in the operating cadence?
options: A full read of the dashboard with notes
Noticing — read alerts, glance at the funnel and active-users trend, dig only if something fired
Reviewing every live journey's send volume
Triaging the experiment backlog
questions.explain: Daily is 2 minutes of noticing; analysis happens weekly and monthly. If the daily glance takes longer, tune the alerts rather than summoning more discipline.

questions.q: In the weekly growth review, which of these belongs on the agenda?
options: A status round where everyone says what they worked on
Debugging the anomaly in the retention curve, live, as a group
Each finished experiment's result and learning, in a sentence, plus the kill-or-scale decisions
Re-litigating the choice of ICE vs PIE scoring
questions.explain: The meeting reviews learnings and decisions, not status. Anomalies get an owner and are debugged outside the meeting; framework debates are explicitly a thing to stop having.

questions.q: Sean Ellis's high-tempo testing argument is that the compounding asset is:
options: The size of each winning experiment
The velocity of tests — learning rate compounds even when individual tests fail
The sophistication of your statistics
The number of channels you test in parallel
questions.explain: More shots on goal means faster learning about what moves YOUR product's numbers. A startup's version of high tempo can be one honest experiment a week, every week.

questions.q: Your test 'won' with 40 users per arm over three days, and the team wants to ship and announce it. The honest read is:
options: Ship it — a win is a win
The sample is almost certainly too small to detect anything real; treat it as a hunch, not a result
Run it again and pick the better of the two results
It counts as long as p < 0.05 at the moment you stopped
questions.explain: Underpowered wins are how experiment theatre starts — at startup traffic, prefer big swings you can actually measure, and treat 'no detectable difference' as a common honest outcome. Peeking-until-significant is p-hacking.

questions.q: The monthly ritual exists because some questions are too slow for the weekly view. Which is a monthly question?
options: Did any alert fire today?
What's this week's cost per conversion?
Is the activation metric still the best predictor of retention, and is the lifecycle program showing incremental lift against the global holdout?
Which experiment launches next from the backlog?
questions.explain: Re-validating the activation metric, reporting holdout lift, and reviewing blended CAC move on month timescales. The weekly meeting would just be staring at noise.

questions.q: A new teammate proposes flipping the order: run ads first to get traffic data, fix retention later. Per the course's ordering, what's wrong with that?
options: Acquisition is the lever that wastes the most money when the bucket leaks — retention work first is what makes later spend pay back
Ads take longer to set up than analytics, so it's simply slower
Nothing — the order is arbitrary as long as all three phases eventually happen
Paid traffic pollutes analytics, so nothing can be measured until the ads are switched off
questions.explain: The leaky bucket from Chapter 2 is why the order is measure → keep → grow and not the reverse. That single ordering decision, agreed as a team on Day 0, is called the most valuable thing in the course.

questions.q: Someone adds 'redesign the pricing page' to the experiment backlog. What does the chapter say it needs before it can be scored?
options: A designer's mockup, so the team has something concrete to react to
Sign-off from the weekly growth review
A switch from ICE to PIE, which handles design changes better
Rewriting as a hypothesis — if we [change], [metric] will move [direction] because [reasoning] — then an ICE score
questions.explain: Ideas enter the backlog as hypotheses, not tasks, so 'this would be huge' has to meet 'how confident are we, actually?'. The score's job is ordering the list — and debating ICE vs PIE is named as the only real mistake.

questions.q: The dashboard shows a retention dip and someone asks which tool will fix it. Under the course's division of labour, the right frame is:
options: PostHog should fix it — it detected the problem
Hogsend should fix it automatically — that's what automation is for
Neither tool decides anything — PostHog detects, Hogsend acts, and a human decides what to do
Whichever tool the growth department owns
questions.explain: The split — PostHog detects, Hogsend acts, you decide — is what keeps the system simple no matter how much you add to it. Kill-or-scale calls are made by people, out loud, in the weekly review.

questions.q: The team wants to A/B test a fix for an error message that's clearly broken. Per the chapter's honest rules, you should:
options: Just ship it — obvious fixes, reversible changes, and changes too small to measure don't earn an experiment's overhead
Test it anyway — a culture of experimentation means testing everything
Add it to the backlog and wait for its ICE score to reach the top
Run it against the global holdout so the lift is provable
questions.explain: Not everything deserves an experiment: experiments earn their overhead on decisions that are expensive to get wrong. At startup traffic that discipline is what keeps tempo honest instead of theatrical.

questions.q: An experiment finishes with no detectable difference and the team moves on without writing anything down. What's the cost the chapter warns about?
options: None — only winning experiments are worth documenting
The result becomes statistically invalid after the fact
The weekly meeting runs over its 30 minutes
Someone re-runs the forgotten experiment later — the thirty-second write-up is what stops the team paying a month to re-learn it
questions.explain: A result only counts once it's recorded — hypothesis, what happened, what you'd try differently. 'No detectable difference' is an honest, common outcome, and a learning that lives in one head is one the team usually pays to re-discover.

cards.front: How rigid are the 30/60/90/180 timings?
cards.back: Phases are gates, not dates — the numbers assume roughly a founder-day per week; compress or stretch freely. Protect the order: each phase's exit criteria are the next phase's entry requirements.

cards.prompt: The day-30 gate — three sayable sentences, with numbers:
cards.cloze: Where {{the funnel loses the most people}}, roughly what {{week-4 retention}} is, and which {{phase of product-market fit}} you're in. Chart volume doesn't count.

cards.front: What's the Measure phase's named anti-pattern?
cards.back: Instrumenting forever. Thirty days is enough to measure a funnel honestly, not to build a data platform — a schema registry in week three is growth work quietly traded for tooling work.

cards.prompt: The Keep-phase build order, by expected impact:
cards.cloze: {{Welcome + onboarding nudge}} first (signup→activation is nearly always the biggest leak), then {{dunning}} (the fastest revenue win in the course), then {{win-back}} for users inactive 14+ days.

cards.front: What's the day-60 gate — when does the bucket hold?
cards.back: Holdout-measured lift on activation or early retention — opens and clicks are activity, lift is proof. The bucket holds water well enough that pouring traffic into it isn't waste.

cards.front: What does the day-90 gate require?
cards.back: Traffic flows from a channel you chose on purpose; conversions are tracked accurately enough to optimise on; and a visible share of new users land in a channel you own — verified email or linked Discord/Telegram.

cards.front: What's the Grow phase's anti-pattern?
cards.back: Scaling spend before measurement is trustworthy — with low EMQ or missing events the platform optimises on noise, and the tuition comes out of your budget. A day on conversion quality beats a week of creative testing.

cards.front: What gates day 180 — and what's the real finish line?
cards.back: Lift and blended CAC reported monthly; major paid step-ups gated on PMF evidence (the Sean Ellis 40% test). The finish line: the plan dissolves into an operating rhythm that no longer needs this chapter.

calc:ch10-paid-readiness.title: Can you afford to open the tap?

questions.q: Your welcome journey's open rate is 62% and you want to report it as proof the lifecycle program works. What's the problem?
options: Open rates are underreported, so the real number is higher
Opens measure sending activity, not lift — without a holdout you can't attribute any retention change to the journey
62% is below the benchmark for welcome emails
Nothing — open rate is the standard lifecycle KPI
questions.explain: The plan's day-60 exit criterion is holdout-measured lift, not engagement metrics. Opens prove the email was sent and rendered; only a held-out comparison group proves the journey caused anything.

questions.q: It's day 45. Activation is leaking badly, but a friend's startup doubled signups with Meta ads and you're tempted to copy them. The plan says:
options: Run the ads — acquisition data will help diagnose activation
Split the budget 50/50 between ads and activation work
Stay in the Keep phase — the day-60 gate (holdout-measured lift) hasn't been passed, so paid traffic would pour into a leaking bucket
Skip to the day-90 gate and run the PMF survey instead
questions.explain: Phases are gates, not dates. Scaling acquisition into a broken activation funnel is the most expensive mistake in the course — you'd be buying churn at ad-market prices.

questions.q: In the 91–180 phase, what does ICE scoring order?
options: Which journeys to prune each quarter
The experiment backlog — by impact, confidence, and ease
Which acquisition channels to fund
Alert thresholds on the daily dashboard
questions.explain: ICE (Sean Ellis) scores growth hypotheses so the weekly ritual always pulls the highest-expected-value test from the top of the backlog instead of the loudest idea in the room.

questions.q: What gates a major step-up in paid spend around day 180?
options: Reaching 10,000 registered users
A blended CAC under $50
Evidence of product-market fit — e.g. the Sean Ellis 40% test
Having all eight lifecycle journeys live
questions.explain: Scaling spend amplifies whatever exists. If under 40% of surveyed users would be 'very disappointed' to lose the product, the money is better spent on the product and the Keep phase than on traffic.

questions.q: The plan's definition of success at day 180 is:
options: Every checklist in this chapter fully ticked
A dedicated growth hire owning the system
North Star growth of at least 10% month over month
The plan dissolved into a weekly operating rhythm — steady test tempo, decisions citing the funnel, learnings reported from failures
questions.explain: The finish line is cultural, not numeric: the machine runs on a cadence that no longer needs the plan. Checklists and hires are means; the operating rhythm is the outcome.

questions.q: It's day 20 of the Measure phase. An engineer proposes pausing the dashboard to build a schema registry and event pipeline 'so the data is right forever'. The plan's read:
options: That's tooling work quietly traded for growth work — thirty days is enough to measure a funnel honestly, not to build a data platform
Good call — trustworthy data is the phase's whole goal, so more infrastructure is better
Fine, as long as the registry ships before the day-30 gate
Skip the dashboard entirely — replays and alerts are enough to exit the phase
questions.explain: The Measure phase's named anti-pattern is instrumenting forever. The exit criteria need a trusted funnel read, not a data platform — a schema registry in week three delays the read without improving it.

questions.q: You're starting the Keep phase with time for exactly one journey this week. For almost every product, the plan says build:
options: Win-back first — dormant users are the most neglected segment
Dunning first — it's the fastest revenue win in the whole course
Welcome + onboarding nudge first — the signup-to-activation gap is nearly always the biggest leak
Whichever journey is easiest to ship, to build momentum
questions.explain: Journeys are ordered by expected impact, and the biggest leak for almost every product sits between signup and activation. Dunning really is the fastest revenue win — which is why it's second, not first.

questions.q: Day 75: your Meta campaign is live, but EMQ is sitting at 3 and some conversion events aren't arriving. Your cofounder wants to triple the budget while momentum is high. The plan says:
options: Triple it — more volume will raise EMQ on its own
Hold spend flat and shift the week to fresh creative testing
Kill paid entirely and pivot to content and launches
Fix conversion quality first — with low EMQ the platform optimises on noise, and the tuition comes out of your budget
questions.explain: The Grow phase's anti-pattern is scaling spend before the measurement is trustworthy. A day spent on conversion quality routinely beats a week of creative testing, because the algorithm can only optimise on what it can see.

questions.q: You're pre-launch with no users yet. How does the plan change?
options: Same sequence — phase 1 becomes 'instrument before launch' and phase 3 becomes launch channels rather than paid scaling
Skip straight to phase 3 — measurement means nothing until you have users
Run phases 1 and 3 in parallel, then add retention work after launch
It doesn't apply until you have product-market fit
questions.explain: The plan is stage-agnostic even though the targets aren't: the one thing worth protecting is the order, because each phase's exit criteria are the next phase's entry requirements.

questions.q: At the day-30 gate review, which of these tells you you're ready to leave the Measure phase?
options: The dashboard has a chart for every event in the tracking plan
You can say, with numbers, where the funnel loses the most people, roughly what week-4 retention is, and which PMF phase you're in
PostHog has collected a full 30 days of data
Every teammate has logged into PostHog at least once
questions.explain: The gate is three sayable sentences, not chart volume — echoing YC's point that a startup's first analytics problem is trusting what the few important charts say, not producing more of them.

questions.q: You're pre-launch with no users yet. In the zero-to-one version of the plan, the Measure phase is mostly:
options: Building the full daily dashboard before launch
Talking to users and instrumenting the one activation signal — quantitative dashboards are noise with no traffic
Running ads to generate enough traffic to measure
Skipping measurement entirely until you have product-market fit
questions.explain: With almost no users, the measurement that matters is qualitative — problem interviews and watching the first people try it — plus instrumenting the single activation event so you can read value the moment traffic arrives. The order holds; the first phase changes shape.

questions.q: A team scaling a working paid channel wants to skip the Measure phase because 'we already have traction'. The plan's read:
options: Fine — traction proves the measurement, so skip to Compound
Measurement only matters before product-market fit
A scaling team that never verified its measurement is optimising on noise — the gates catch the stage-appropriate version of the same mistake
Skip Measure but repeat the Keep phase instead
questions.explain: The plan is stage-agnostic in order, not in weighting. Don't skip a phase because your stage lets you feel past it: a scaling team's Measure phase is a tune-up, not a build, but skipping it entirely means optimising spend on numbers nobody checked.

questions.q: Your weekly growth review only ever reports winning experiments. According to the plan, the risk is:
options: None — winners are the point of a review
It quietly teaches the team to stop taking risks, and the learning rate is the thing that compounds
You'll run out of ideas to test
The review will run over 30 minutes
questions.explain: A review where only wins get airtime trains people to avoid risk. A team that can name last week's most useful failed test without embarrassment is running the loop the way it's meant to run — the losers carry the learning.

questions.q: You have one paid channel working, with a known CAC and payback. When does the plan say to add a second channel?
options: Immediately, to reduce the risk of depending on one platform
Only once the first is measured and profitable — then add the next deliberately and hold it to the same gate
Never — focus on the one that works
Only after you've made your first growth hire
questions.explain: A single channel is a real single point of failure, but adding one before the first is understood just splits attention across two things you can't yet read. The signal to diversify is a stable, known CAC and payback on the first — then the second earns its place through the same gate.

calc:ch11-pmf-40.title: The 40% PMF test — are you above the bar?

calc:ch11-paid-readiness.title: Can you actually afford this channel yet?

flashcards:ch11-cards-metrics-that-lie.title: Key ideas — the metrics that lie by stage

cards.prompt: Which number to distrust at each stage:
cards.cloze: Pre-launch — {{waitlist / landing-page signups}}. Just launched — {{the signup count}}. Finding PMF — {{revenue without retention}}. Early traction — {{blended CAC}}. Scaling — {{engagement without holdout-measured lift}}.

cards.front: Pre-launch, why does waitlist size mislead?
cards.back: An email address costs nothing to give, so a big list measures curiosity, not demand. The real pre-launch signal is an observed problem and people already hacking a workaround — demand that existed before you showed up.

cards.front: Just after launch, why is the signup count a vanity metric?
cards.back: Signups measure hands raised, not value received. A launch-day spike of people who never come back is activity with good timing. Watch the share of new users who reach first value (activation) instead.

cards.front: In the PMF hunt, how can revenue lie?
cards.back: MRR can climb while the existing base churns underneath — new sales paper over a leaky bucket. Revenue is a lagging score; read it alongside a retention curve, not instead of one, or you'll scale a product that doesn't hold.

cards.front: Why is blended CAC dangerous in early traction?
cards.back: Blending cheap organic acquisition with expensive paid averages the paid cost down, so paid looks affordable when it isn't. Break CAC out per channel before you decide a channel is scalable — the blend hides the truth about the one you'd pour money into.

cards.front: At scale, why don't opens and engagement prove a journey works?
cards.back: They measure activity, not lift. Without a held-out comparison group you can't attribute any retention change to the journey (Chapter 5). A high open rate proves the email sent and rendered — nothing about cause.

cards.front: Why isn't a rising DAU chart proof that users stay?
cards.back: Aggregate DAU can climb purely on fresh acquisition while every cohort still decays — the line rises because you're pouring faster, not because the bucket holds. Cohort retention curves, not total DAU, tell you if users actually stick.

cards.prompt: The tell for vanity vs. actionable metrics:
cards.cloze: A number is probably vanity if it {{only ever goes up}}, {{can't be tied to a decision}}, or measures {{activity instead of an outcome}}. Actionable metrics are {{rates}}, are comparable over time, and change what you'd do next.

questions.q: You read a Series B company's essay on its referral loop and growth-team org chart and want to copy the whole thing. You have 200 users and shaky retention. The best read is:
options: Copy it — a proven playbook de-risks your growth
Copy the org chart now so you're ready when you scale
It's the answer to a constraint that company had later; your binding constraint is retention, so their playbook spends your time on the wrong problem
Ignore all later-stage content until you're profitable
questions.explain: A lever is good or bad for your stage. Published playbooks are written by survivors, after the fact — running one against a constraint you don't have yet burns the resource you can't refill, time, on the wrong problem.

questions.q: Pre-launch, a viral tweet lands you a 3,000-email waitlist. Your cofounder calls it proof of demand and wants to build the full product. The honest read:
options: 3,000 is strong demand — build with confidence
An email costs nothing to give, so a waitlist measures curiosity, not demand — validate the problem in real conversations first
Double down on the tweet's channel immediately
Run paid ads to the list to warm it up
questions.explain: Waitlist size is a classic pre-launch vanity metric. 'No market need' sits at or near the top of startup post-mortems (directional) — the fix is observed demand, not a bigger list.

questions.q: Two weeks after launch you have 1,200 signups and a great launch-day chart, but haven't checked how many did anything afterward. What matters most now?
options: The signup total — momentum is the leading indicator
Reaching 5,000 signups before optimising anything
The share of those signups who reached first value — a spike that never returns is activity, not traction
Adding far more event tracking before drawing any conclusion
questions.explain: Activation is the just-launched metric. Celebrating the signup spike and freezing to over-instrument are the two traps of this stage — one number acted on beats forty you don't read.

questions.q: You survey 100 active users; 28 say they'd be 'very disappointed' to lose the product. A friend urges you to triple ad spend while you have momentum. The stage says:
options: 28% is close enough — scale now while it's hot
You're under the ~40% bar, so spend is better on the product and retention until the pull is real — scaling only amplifies a take-it-or-leave-it product
Re-run the survey until it clears 40%
The 40% test only applies to consumer apps, so ignore it here
questions.explain: Sean Ellis' 40% is a rule-of-thumb gate on the tap. Scaling acquisition before fit is the most expensive mistake in the course — paid amplifies whatever exists, and 28% is a product people can still take or leave.

questions.q: MRR is up 20% this quarter, but every monthly cohort's retention curve still decays toward zero by week 8. Have you found product-market fit?
options: Yes — revenue growth is the definition of fit
No — revenue is rising on new sales while the base churns; a curve that decays to zero has no plateau, so acquisition won't save it
Yes, as long as MRR keeps outpacing churn
Can't say without running a 40% survey
questions.explain: A retention curve that flattens above zero is fit expressed as data. Revenue without retention is a leaky bucket masked by fresh sales — read the curve alongside MRR, never instead of it.

questions.q: You've found fit. You're now running Google Ads, TikTok, SEO, cold email, and a partnership — each for about two weeks — and none is clearly working. The best move:
options: Add two more channels to widen the net
Conclude paid doesn't work for you and go organic-only
Pick the one or two most promising, commit long enough to climb the learning curve, and prove one before adding a second
Split the budget evenly across all five permanently
questions.explain: Channel-hopping teaches almost nothing — every channel has a learning curve, and quitting early reads as 'it didn't work' when the truth is 'I didn't finish.' Depth on one beats breadth across many in early traction.

questions.q: Your blended CAC is $40 and looks great, so you plan to 5× the paid budget. But most of that $40 is cheap organic — paid alone costs $180 per customer. The risk:
options: None — blended CAC is the number investors use
Scaling paid will lift organic too, keeping the blend low
The blend hides that paid costs $180; 5×ing paid scales the expensive channel, so true CAC and cash burn are far worse than $40 suggests
You should just raise prices instead of worrying about CAC
questions.explain: Blended CAC lies precisely when you're about to scale one channel. Averaging cheap organic in with expensive paid makes paid look affordable — break CAC out per channel before you pour money into it.

questions.q: You're scaling and about to make your first dedicated growth hire. Which brief is right?
options: 'Go find our growth channel and figure out product-market fit'
'Own and accelerate the working loop — the funnel, the experiment backlog, and the lifecycle system'
'Rebuild our analytics from scratch before doing anything else'
'Run the exact playbook of the big company we admire'
questions.explain: Hire into a working loop, not to invent one. A growth hire inherits a machine to run faster; asking them to find fit or a first channel is asking them to do an earlier stage's job.

questions.q: At scale your team ships a new onboarding journey and reports a 55% open rate as proof it lifted retention, while 'cumulative signups keep going up.' What's wrong with the evidence?
options: 55% is below the open-rate benchmark for onboarding email
Opens measure activity, not lift — without a holdout you can't attribute retention to the journey; and cumulative signups only ever rise, so they prove nothing
Nothing — open rate and total signups are the standard scaling KPIs
The journey should have been built at launch, not at scale
questions.explain: Activity isn't lift. Only a held-out comparison group attributes a retention change to the journey (Chapter 5), and a cumulative total that can only go up is the definition of a vanity metric.

questions.q: Across the whole chapter, the single recurring mistake being warned against is:
options: Not instrumenting enough events
Running a later stage's playbook against an earlier stage's constraint — scaling spend before fit, hiring for growth before the loop works, treating a waitlist as demand
Choosing the wrong analytics tool
Surveying users too frequently
questions.explain: The same lever helps at one stage and hurts at another. The through-line is: name the stage you're actually in, then solve that stage's binding constraint instead of borrowing a stage you haven't reached.

cards.front: Why isn't running an experiment free?
cards.back: Every test costs traffic (split, so each variant learns slower), time (build, wait for significance, read), and opportunity (what you didn't ship meanwhile). Testing buys certainty — worth it only when the certainty is worth more than the cost.

cards.prompt: The experiment hypothesis format:
cards.cloze: We believe {{change}} for {{audience}} will cause {{outcome}}, measured by {{metric}}, because {{reasoning}} — the last slot is the belief you're actually testing.

cards.front: Primary metric vs guardrail metrics?
cards.back: One primary metric (Kohavi's OEC), chosen before the test, decides success. Guardrails are the short list you refuse to break whatever the primary does — revenue, unsubscribe rate, latency, retention. Watching every metric guarantees a false 'win'.

cards.front: Why is an underpowered 'win' worse than no test?
cards.back: Low power both misses real effects and hands you false wins that won't replicate. Acting on a false win is worse than not testing — you ship the change and trust it. Required sample grows with ~the square of the effect you want to detect.

cards.front: What does a holdout prove that an open rate can't?
cards.back: Incremental lift — the outcome that happened because of the program and wouldn't have anyway. Opens are activity; lift is proof. A persistent global holdout (1–5% of users) reads the whole lifecycle program's real contribution.

cards.front: The multiple-comparisons trap?
cards.back: Test enough metrics or slices and ~1 in 20 looks significant at 95% confidence by pure chance. Slice a result enough ways and you always find a 'winner'. The committed primary metric decides the test; everything else is a hypothesis for next time.

cards.prompt: Every experiment ends in one of three decisions:
cards.cloze: {{ship}} it (clear win, guardrails intact), {{kill}} it (flat or negative), or {{iterate}} (promising but inconclusive — sharpen and rerun) — then write down the hypothesis, result, and what's next.

cards.front: What actually compounds in an experimentation program?
cards.back: The learning rate, not any single win (Sean Ellis, high-tempo testing). An ICE-scored backlog plus a steady weekly tempo — one test launched or reported each week, results written down — beats a burst of ten then a quiet quarter.

questions.q: A one-word change to a button label looks obviously clearer, and you can revert it in a minute. Your traffic is low. The best move is:
options: Run a two-week A/B test to be sure before shipping
Ship it — it's obvious, cheap, and reversible; save testing capacity for expensive, hard-to-undo decisions
Test it, but peek daily and ship as soon as it's significant
Add it to the backlog and ICE-score it against everything else
questions.explain: Experiments cost traffic, time, and opportunity. Obvious, reversible, low-cost changes should just ship; testing earns its overhead on decisions that are expensive to get wrong. At low traffic a change this small probably can't be detected anyway.

questions.q: 'Our best engineers know what works — experiments just slow us down.' The strongest rebuttal is:
options: Experiments are fast now, so speed isn't a real objection
At Microsoft only about a third of tested ideas moved the metric as intended — experts routinely can't predict winners, which is the whole reason to test the expensive ones
A/B testing is an industry standard, so you should do it too
Engineers shouldn't be deciding product questions
questions.explain: Kohavi's data: ~1/3 of ideas win, 1/3 do nothing, 1/3 hurt. The Bing headline change worth ~$100M/year sat ignored for months because it looked trivial. If intuition were reliable you wouldn't need the test; because it isn't, testing catches the costly misses.

questions.q: In 'We believe [change] for [audience] will cause [outcome], measured by [metric], because [reasoning]', which slot teaches you the most when the test fails?
options: The change — you learn what to build next
The metric — you learn what to measure
The reasoning ('because') — it's the belief being tested, so a failure tells you the belief was wrong, not just that a number didn't move
The audience — you learn who to target
questions.explain: The 'because' slot is the hypothesis's belief. A test pinned to a belief teaches you something even when it fails; a test with no reasoning leaves you a number and no idea what it meant — which is how teams re-run forgotten failures.

questions.q: A teammate proposes judging each test on 'whichever of our fifteen metrics improves.' The problem:
options: Fifteen metrics is too few to be reliable
Some metric is always up by chance, so you'll 'win' almost every test — you need one primary metric chosen before the test, plus a few guardrails
Nothing — more metrics means more rigour
It's fine as long as revenue is one of the fifteen
questions.explain: Declaring victory when any metric is up is the multiple-comparisons trap. One primary metric (the OEC), committed up front, decides the test; guardrails are the few you refuse to break. More metrics feels rigorous and is the opposite.

questions.q: You're testing an onboarding change in a two-week experiment. Which primary metric is the best choice?
options: 30-day retention — it's the truest measure of value
Annual revenue per user
Week-one activation rate — a leading indicator sensitive enough to move inside the test window, and tied to the change
Lifetime value
questions.explain: Revenue, LTV, and 30-day retention are true but slow — they barely move in two weeks, so the test would be underpowered on them. Pick a leading indicator that can actually shift in the window and that the change plausibly drives.

questions.q: Detecting a 2% lift needs a certain amount of traffic. Roughly how much do you need to detect a 1% lift instead?
options: Half as much — smaller effects are easier
About the same
Twice as much
About four times as much — required sample grows with roughly the square of how small an effect you want to catch
questions.explain: Sample size scales with ~1/MDE². Halving the effect you want to detect roughly quadruples the traffic needed. That's why small teams should test big swings, not tiny tweaks — small effects are enormously expensive to prove.

questions.q: A test is set to run two weeks. On day 4 it crosses 95% significance and someone wants to call it and ship. The right response:
options: Ship — significance is significance, whenever it appears
Wait for the pre-committed sample size; stopping the moment it looks significant (peeking) inflates the false-positive rate, and the p-value isn't trustworthy yet
Ship, but keep the test running in the background
Extend the test to a month to be safe
questions.explain: Peeking and early-stopping badly inflate false positives — a null test touches significance surprisingly often if you check daily. The p-value is only valid at the sample size you committed to. Decide the finish line first, then read it there.

questions.q: Your welcome journey has a 62% open rate and you want to prove the lifecycle program drives retention. What actually proves it?
options: A higher open rate on the next send
A persistent holdout — a small group kept out of the program entirely — showing incremental lift over doing nothing
Click-through rate on the emails
The number of journeys live
questions.explain: Opens are activity; lift is proof. Open and click rates can't separate 'the journey worked' from 'these users would have returned anyway.' Only a held-out control reveals the counterfactual — the program's incremental contribution.

questions.q: Your experiment comes back flat — no detectable difference. The best move:
options: Delete it; a non-result isn't worth recording
Re-slice the data until some segment shows a win, then ship to that segment
Record it as a finding — the effect is smaller than hoped — decide kill or iterate, and note why for the backlog
Rerun it immediately with the same design until it wins
questions.explain: About two-thirds of ideas don't win, so flat results are most of the job. 'No detectable difference' is a real finding that saves you from shipping a change and believing it worked. Re-slicing until something's significant is p-hacking; a documented failure is what a growth team can say out loud.

questions.q: Your team can realistically run one experiment a week. A colleague argues you should instead spend a month designing one 'perfect' high-stakes test. The program's view:
options: Agree — one rigorous test beats several rough ones
One test a week, every week, with results written down, compounds faster — velocity of tests is the asset, and you can't predict which will be the outsized winner
Do neither; wait until you have more traffic
Run ten tests this week to catch up, then pause
questions.explain: Sean Ellis's high-tempo argument: the learning rate compounds, not any single win, and nobody reliably predicts the winner in advance. Steady cadence beats bursts — one a week every week outperforms ten then a quiet quarter. Rigour and tempo aren't opposites; the weekly ritual enforces both.

cards.prompt: The four audiences and the altitude each cares about:
cards.cloze: {{Investors/board}}: a few outcome ratios and the trend. {{Cofounders}}: the whole picture, unpolished. {{The team}}: input metrics they can move this week. {{Customers}}: the outcome for them, not your numbers.

cards.front: Why does a single number never persuade?
cards.back: Alone it can't show cause — it invites 'so what?'. The persuasive unit is a pair: a leading metric you control and the lagging outcome it moved, with the mechanism stated between them.

cards.prompt: The two cadences and what each reports:
cards.cloze: {{Weekly team review}}: operational — input metrics, journeys and spend, experiments, decisions not status. {{Monthly/board update}}: outcomes — North Star trend, retention, lift, CAC, and one learning.

cards.front: How do you explain a holdout to a skeptic without a stats lecture?
cards.back: Use the placebo intuition: 'we held back a random slice and sent them nothing; the messaged group beat them by X points; that gap is what the journey added.' Name the gap as the credit, and say what would have changed your mind.

cards.front: Why is a losing experiment worth presenting well?
cards.back: Most experiments lose — only ~a third improve their target metric even at Microsoft (Kohavi). A loss is a learning you already paid for; hiding it wastes it. State the hypothesis, say what it ruled out, show your tempo.

cards.front: What is a vanity metric, and what replaces it?
cards.back: A number that reliably rises and informs no decision — cumulative totals, impressions, registered users. Replace it with a rate or a cohort (something that can go down), and apply the 'so what?' test (Eric Ries, The Lean Startup).

cards.front: What's the long-run cost of over-claiming attribution?
cards.back: The first hero story that doesn't survive a holdout discounts every future number you present. Credibility is priced by your least defensible claim — so say the smaller, defensible number and name what you can't prove.

cards.prompt: The monthly one-pager — four blocks and a sentence:
cards.cloze: {{North Star + trend}}, {{activation + retention}}, {{blended CAC + referral contribution}}, {{one experiment learning}} — plus one sentence: {{the risk you're most worried about}}.

questions.q: Your board asks how growth is going, so you forward them the weekly team review — every input metric, every experiment, the whole operational agenda. What's the problem?
options: Nothing — more detail always builds a board's trust
You've handed the highest-altitude audience the lowest-altitude data; a board wants a trend and a few outcome ratios with one narrative, not your operational dashboard
The weekly review isn't detailed enough for a board
Board updates should contain only revenue
questions.explain: Different audiences read at different altitudes, and one report for all of them fails. The board's job is a trend and a small set of outcome ratios; the operational input metrics are the team's report, not theirs.

questions.q: A stakeholder is unmoved by 'activation is 44%.' The most persuasive fix is to:
options: Show that 44% beats the industry benchmark
Present a bigger cumulative number instead
Pair it with the input you moved and the outcome that followed — 'we shipped the checklist, completion went 51→68%, and that cohort's week-4 retention is +3 points'
Add three more metrics to the slide
questions.explain: A single number can't show cause, so it invites 'so what?'. The persuasive unit is a leading metric you controlled plus the lagging outcome it moved, with the mechanism stated between them.

questions.q: Which of these belongs in the monthly board update but NOT the weekly team review?
options: Which experiment launches next from the backlog
Each live journey's send volume this week
Blended CAC and the referral loop's contribution to growth
The anomaly an owner is investigating this week
questions.explain: Weekly is operational — inputs, journeys and spend, experiments, this week's work. Monthly is outcomes and trend: North Star trend, lift, blended CAC, and one learning. CAC economics are a monthly-altitude number.

questions.q: A skeptical investor asks how you know the welcome journey caused the retention bump. The explanation most likely to land is:
options: 'We ran a two-tailed t-test and the p-value came in under 0.05'
'Open rate was 62%, well above the benchmark'
'We held back a random 10% who got nothing; the messaged group upgraded 7 points higher; that gap is what the journey added'
'Retention went up the month we launched it'
questions.explain: Lead with the control group (the placebo intuition), name the gap as the credit, and skip the statistics vocabulary. The last option is correlation, not proof; the first loses the room.

questions.q: Your second-onboarding-email test came back flat. The best way to present it is to:
options: Leave it out of the report — a flat result isn't worth the airtime
Dig through segments until you find one where it won, and report that
State the hypothesis, report it flat, and say what it ruled out — 'the activation leak isn't email volume, it's earlier'
Report it as a small win to keep morale up
questions.explain: A loss is a learning you already paid for; hiding it — or p-hacking a win out of it — is the fastest way to lose a numbers-literate stakeholder. State the hypothesis and what the flat result ruled out.

questions.q: What does Kohavi's Microsoft data — only about a third of well-designed experiments improve their target metric — imply for how you talk about experiments?
options: A losing test means the team designed it wrong
A normal loop produces mostly losses, so present tempo and learnings, not just wins — losing is the expected output of working experimentation
You should stop testing until your win rate is above 50%
Experiments are too unreliable to report to a board
questions.explain: If the best experimentation teams win only about a third of the time, most of your results will be losses too. Velocity compounds because tests fail; the report should show the tempo and the learnings, not hide the losses.

questions.q: Which of these numbers best survives the 'so what?' test?
options: Total registered users since launch
Impressions on last week's launch tweet
Week-4 retention for the cohort that joined in May, vs April
Cumulative GMV to date
questions.explain: Vanity metrics are cumulative totals and raw reach that can only rise. A cohort retention rate can go down, and a change in it changes a decision — which is exactly what makes it actionable rather than decorative.

questions.q: Your cofounder wants to tell investors 'our Product Hunt launch drove 10,000 signups' and triple ad spend on that basis. The chapter's read is:
options: Report it as-is — last-click numbers are the standard
That's a last-click hero story claiming a correlational number as causal; any number used to justify more spend is exactly the one to run incrementality on first
Halve the spend increase instead, to be safe
Drop paid entirely and rely on launches
questions.explain: What a channel reports and what it actually added over doing nothing are different numbers. Over-claiming discounts every future claim, and spend-justifying numbers should be held to the holdout bar.

questions.q: Which set belongs on the monthly one-pager?
options: Total signups, emails sent, pageviews, follower count
North Star trend, activation and retention, blended CAC plus referral share, and one experiment learning
Every chart on the daily dashboard
A list of every experiment run this quarter
questions.explain: The one-pager is four blocks and a sentence — outcomes and trend, one learning, and the risk you're worried about. Cumulative totals and raw reach are exactly what it leaves off.

questions.q: Why put 'the one thing you're worried about' on a board update?
options: It fills space when the results are thin
Boards are legally required to see a risks section
Naming the risk you'd want help on builds more trust than a flawless slide — it's the difference between reporting to a board and working with it
It lowers expectations so any result looks good
questions.explain: Surfacing a real risk signals you aren't managing the numbers for the audience — which is precisely what makes the good numbers on the same page believable.

flashcards:ch14-cards-roles.title: Key ideas — reading a growth role

cards.prompt: The three shapes of a growth role, by where they sit on the loop:
cards.cloze: {{Growth marketer}} leans on acquisition and top-of-funnel; {{growth PM}} owns a funnel slice as a product surface (activation, retention, monetisation); {{growth engineer}} builds the instrumentation and experimentation machinery.

cards.front: Why read the responsibilities, not the title?
cards.back: The same title means different jobs at different companies — a data-driven loop-owner at one, a cold-email operator at another. The verbs in the responsibilities tell you which stage of the loop you'd actually own.

cards.front: What's the single non-negotiable green flag?
cards.back: Data access. If the role owns a metric, you must be able to see and instrument it. A posting that names the analytics stack is telling you the loop is real.

cards.front: Which green flag separates a real loop from a channel job?
cards.back: Experimentation as a norm — A/B tests, holdouts, a backlog — plus retention language, not just 'drive signups'. It signals a team that knows activity from lift.

cards.front: The clearest red flag in a growth JD?
cards.back: Growth = cold outreach only, with no analytics — a sales-development job relabelled. Close behind: no mention of data or measurement anywhere in the responsibilities.

cards.front: What does an all-acquisition, no-retention posting tell you?
cards.back: It's the leaky-bucket team from Chapter 2 hiring someone to pour faster. Everything is top-of-funnel; nothing about activation or retention. You'd be scaling into a leak.

cards.front: Which door tends to fit an engineer coming from this course?
cards.back: Growth engineer — building instrumentation, experimentation tooling, and the internal surfaces that let the loop run fast. Though a growth PM role rewards the whole-loop thinking this course taught too.

questions.q: An interviewer asks about your best growth work. Which framing does an experienced interviewer score highest?
options: 'We ran a referral push and signups went up 20%' — a clear, concrete result
'I saw activation leaking, shipped an onboarding journey, held out 10% to measure it, saw a few points of retention lift against the holdout, then moved to the next-biggest leak'
'I own our entire growth funnel end to end and drive all our key metrics'
'I ran about a dozen growth experiments last quarter across every channel'
questions.explain: That answer reads as a loop, not a project: a system, a control group, measured lift, and a decision about where to go next. The 20% story has no baseline; the others assert scope or count without showing measurement judgement.

questions.q: You increased retention while an onboarding journey was live, but a pricing change and a seasonal bump landed the same month. How should you describe your impact in an interview?
options: 'I increased retention 40%' — round up, confidence sells
Don't mention it — if you can't claim it cleanly, leave it out
'Retention rose while my journey ran; several things moved at once, so I'd trust the holdout comparison over the raw number, and I'd want a repeat before calling it durable'
Attribute all of the lift to your journey — you shipped it, so it's yours
questions.explain: Attribution humility wins. Pairing the claim with how you'd know — and naming what you're still unsure of — reads as someone who's been accountable for a number. Over-claiming a clean figure with confounders present is the fastest way to lose a numerate interviewer.

questions.q: A job posting titled 'Growth Lead' describes the role entirely as cold outbound volume, with no mention of analytics, metrics, or experimentation. What is it, most likely?
options: A strong growth-PM role — outbound is a legitimate growth channel
A sales-development job relabelled 'growth' — a red flag, not a growth loop
A growth-engineering role, since outreach can be automated
Impossible to tell without applying
questions.explain: Growth = cold outreach only, with no data or measurement anywhere in the responsibilities, is the clearest red flag in the chapter. Read the verbs, not the title: a growth role without data access or experimentation is a growth role in name only.

questions.q: An interview ends with 'what would your first 90 days here look like?' What makes the strongest answer?
options: Bold targets and a specific tactic for each stage, promising a number by day 30
A plan that leads with what you'd instrument and look at first, sequences retention before acquisition, and sets gates you can be held to
A promise to drive growth aggressively from week one
Declining to answer until you have access to their internal data
questions.explain: The Chapter 10 shape — learn the data first, early wins in activation/retention, then scale — is the deliverable this question asks for. A confident plan for a funnel you've never seen reads as someone who acts before they look; leading with measurement reads as maturity.

questions.q: Why does the chapter call your course workbook a portfolio rather than just study notes?
options: Because it's long, and length signals effort to a hiring manager
Because it records the full loop — instrumented funnel, shipped journeys, holdout results, a plan — with your reasoning attached, which is evidence of judgement a résumé bullet can't carry
Because portfolios are required for growth roles
Because it proves you completed a paid course
questions.explain: A portfolio beats an assertion because it shows judgement, not just claimed outcomes. The workbook holds your reasoning at each step — including results that didn't work — which is exactly what an interviewer can't get from a bullet point.

questions.q: Two candidates answer 'how would you grow this product?'. One lists ten growth tactics; the other says 'first I'd want a holdout before claiming any of that worked'. Who tends to score higher, and why?
options: The tactics list — it shows breadth and energy
The second — measurement judgement beats buzzwords, because they'll be trusted to spend money and report on it honestly
They score the same; it's a matter of interviewer taste
The tactics list, as long as the tactics are current
questions.explain: In every growth-interview question type, judgement about measurement outscores a pile of tactics. The holdout answer signals someone who's been accountable for a number and won't fool the team or themselves.

questions.q: The chapter borrows 'career capital' from Cal Newport. What's the core claim?
options: Follow your passion first, and the skills will come
Rare, valuable skills are the capital you trade for autonomy and better work — and mastery usually comes before passion, not after
Career capital means saving enough money to take risks
The more job titles you hold, the more capital you accumulate
questions.explain: Newport's argument in So Good They Can't Ignore You runs against 'follow your passion': you build rare and valuable skills, then trade that capital for control and interesting problems. A full-loop growth capability is exactly that kind of rare skill.

questions.q: The chapter applies the course's owned-audience lesson to your career. What's the payoff of a small owned audience?
options: Nothing until it's large — reach is the only thing that counts
It's a compounding, self-owned flywheel: a handful of the right people who've seen your work is worth more than a big anonymous following, and it doesn't depend on a recruiter's inbox
It replaces the need for a portfolio or interviews
It only helps if you want to become a full-time content creator
questions.explain: The owned-audience-over-vanity-metric lesson from Chapter 8, pointed at you: you own the relationship, it compounds while you sleep, and it differentiates you before an interview starts. A few of the right people beats a big anonymous number.

questions.q: Which question, asked of an interviewer, best signals that you think like a growth operator?
options: 'What's the salary band and equity for this role?'
'How many people are on the growth team?'
'Is the product instrumented, will I have data access, and who owns the growth loop today?'
'What's the tech stack?'
questions.explain: Operator questions probe the conditions you'd need to do the job — data access is non-negotiable for anyone owning a metric — and their answers also tell you whether the role is a real loop or a growth job in name only.

questions.q: What single distinction is the chapter most concerned that you can demonstrate?
options: Knowing more growth tactics than the next candidate
The difference between running a growth project once and running a growth loop — shown through systems thinking, measurement, and incrementality
Having worked at a well-known company
Fluency in the latest growth tooling
questions.explain: Project-vs-loop is the distinction strong interviewers probe for, and every other piece in the chapter serves it: the portfolio, the STAR story, the 90-day plan, and attribution humility all exist to make your work read as a loop you run, not a project you ran once.
